description: v4.5.2A - Module 6 expansion (+8). Total 542 scenarios: 251 M1 (board texture) + 49 M2 (flop c-bet IP) + 85 M3 (flop defense OOP) + 92 M4 (turn defense OOP) + 33 M5 (river defense OOP) + 32 M6 (river betting IP). M6 = BTN river bet/check decision IP after BB checks (seat mirror of M5), schemaVersion 1.4.0 per-scenario (verdictBasis + stakeBasis owner PINs; R94-R107 production rules incl. the boat-or-better recompute lint). Lives in Tournament at Final Table depth via the pf_river_value_ip hook; curriculum wire follows in v4.5.3. Spot context: BTN open 2.5x vs BB call, 100BB SRP, NLH MTT chipEV.

question.prompt: On Ah Kd 5c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN. Open range contains far more A-x and K-x; BB 3-bets most strong A-x preflop.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN open ~45% range includes A2s-AKs, A8o-AKo, K9s+, KJo+ — many cards that connect with A or K. BB calling range vs BTN open is roughly 30% (22-JJ, suited connectors, suited gappers, A2s-A9s, KTs/KJs, QJs, etc.). Critically, BB 3-bets AJo+, AQs, AKs, KQs, KJs at high frequency, removing them from the flatting range. Result: BTN's flop range is much more A-x-heavy and K-x-heavy.
explanation.nutLogic: BTN holds AA, KK, AK at full preflop frequency; BB 3-bets these heavily, so BB's flatting range contains far fewer combos. Top of range overwhelmingly favors BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Some players answer 'neutral' assuming pocket pairs and connectors balance things out. They don't — sets of 5 are rare in both ranges and BTN has many more pairs that connect with A and K specifically.

question.prompt: On Kh Td 2s, who has nut advantage?

explanation.short: BTN. KK and AK dominate the top of range; BB 3-bets these often so they're sparse in BB's flatting range.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN open contains KK, KJo, KJs, KTs, KTo (some), KQ, AK at high frequency. BB calls vs BTN typically with 22-JJ and suited Broadways, but BB 3-bets KK, AK, KQs, KQo at high frequency.
explanation.nutLogic: Top of range hands on Kh Td 2s are sets (KK, TT, 22), top two (KT), top pair top kicker (AK, KQ). BTN dominates KK and AK. TT is in both ranges but BB's KT combos are rare (KTo / KTs typically 3-bet or fold). 22 is in BB more often. Net nut advantage clearly BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Some assume 'TT and 22 are easier for BB to have' and answer 'caller' — but the dominant top-of-range hands (KK, AK) are heavily in BTN's range and outweigh the small-pair edge.

question.prompt: On Jh Ts 9c (highly connected), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Caller (BB). Highly connected board favors BB's wider connector / suited-gapper range.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB's calling range contains many JTs, T9s, 98s, 87s, J9s, T8s, 76s — all of which hit this board for top pair, two pair, sets, or straights. BTN opens these but at lower frequency in raw combos than the broader BB call range. BB also has 88, 77, 66, 55 etc. for sets and pair-plus-draw equity.
explanation.nutLogic: Made straights (KQ, Q8, 87, 78) and two-pair combos (JT, T9, J9) appear at higher frequency in BB's range. BTN does have AA-QQ overpairs, but on this connected board overpairs are not nutted — they're often only marginal made hands.
explanation.commonMistake: BTN players often assume 'I raised, so I have range advantage' and over-c-bet here. That's a major leak — solver play has BTN c-betting only ~25 — 35% of range here, often with polar sizings, because the board belongs to BB.

question.prompt: On 5h 4d 3c (low connected), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Caller (BB). BB's range is dense in low pairs, low suited connectors, and made straights.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB calls vs BTN with 22, 33, 44, 55, 66, 77, plus 76s, 65s, 54s, 43s (some), A2s-A5s, etc. Many of these make sets, two pair, top pair, or open-ended on this board. BTN's overpairs (TT-AA) are a thin slice of range and aren't even ahead of BB's many made hands.
explanation.nutLogic: Sets (33, 44, 55) are concentrated in BB. Made straights (76, 67, A2 for the wheel) are mostly in BB's range. BTN's nut advantage is essentially zero here — even AA is just an overpair on a heavily textured low board.
explanation.commonMistake: Big leak: BTN players who think 'low board = no one has it = always c-bet small' get crushed here. The whole BB calling range loves this board. Solver: BTN checks ~70%+ of range.

question.prompt: On Ah Ad 6s (paired ace), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN. With one of the two remaining aces, BTN's wider opening range contains far more trip-A combos.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN open includes A2s-AKs and A8o-AKo. BB calling range has only A2s-A9s and very few off-suit aces (BB 3-bets AJo+, AQo+). With 2 aces on the board, only 2 unseen aces remain — and BTN has many more A-x combos to hold one of them.
explanation.nutLogic: Trips-A is the nut equivalent here. BTN holds it at much higher frequency. Full house combos (66, AA — but AA is impossible with 2 aces on board) are essentially the same low frequency for both players, and 66 is mostly in BB's range. Net, BTN dominates.
explanation.commonMistake: Some players overweight 'BB has 66 = BB has nuts' and answer caller. Sets of 6 are 3 combos for BB and 3 for BTN — they cancel. The real edge is in trip-A frequency, which BTN dominates.

question.prompt: On 8h 7c 6s, how dynamic is this board?

explanation.short: Very dynamic. Every overcard makes a straight or massively shifts equity.
explanation.rangeLogic: Made straights already exist (T9, 95, 54). The turn or river: 5-straight for 9-x and 4-x; 9-straight for 5-x and T-x; T-straight for J-x and 9-x; J-straight for T-x; 4-straight for 5-x; etc. Almost any non-paired card changes the equity landscape significantly.
explanation.commonMistake: Underestimating how many cards swing equity. The rule of thumb: if an overcard or undercard makes a straight for a meaningful slice of the calling range, the board is at least dynamic. Here, it's about as dynamic as flops get.

question.prompt: On 9c 5d 2h (low dry rainbow), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN — slight to moderate edge. Overpairs (TT-AA) dominate this static, disconnected low board.
explanation.rangeLogic: On 9-5-2 disconnected rainbow, the strongest hand class is overpairs. BTN has TT, JJ, QQ, KK, AA in opening range. BB has TT-QQ for sure, KK and AA at much lower frequency (3-bets these often). BTN also has many overcards (A-x, K-x) that have backdoor + over-pair-when-paired equity.
explanation.nutLogic: Sets (99, 55, 22) are about equally distributed. Two-pair (95, 92, 52) is rare in both ranges. The nut hands here are sets and overpairs; BTN's overpair density gives the edge.
explanation.commonMistake: Some players answer 'caller' assuming 'BB has small pairs and 5x'. But BB has roughly the same set frequency, and BTN's overpair density wins on this static board.

question.prompt: On Kh Kd 7s (paired king), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN — large edge. BTN has many more K-x combos for trips, plus overpairs.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN open contains K9s+, KJo+, KQ at high frequency. BB's flatting range vs BTN open contains very few K-x combos because BB 3-bets KJ+ and KQs/KQo most of the time. With 2 kings on the board, the trips-K combos remaining go heavily to BTN.
explanation.nutLogic: Trips-K is the dominant top-of-range hand. BTN dominates trips-K. KK is impossible. 77 is in BB much more often (BTN often opens 77 but BB has it as a flat combo more frequently). Boats from KK would matter but KK is on the board. Net: BTN has clear nut advantage too.
explanation.commonMistake: Underweighting the impact of BB 3-betting K-x preflop. The 'BB has random K' myth fails on board where BB filtered most K-x out preflop.

question.prompt: On Ah Kd 5d (two-tone), what is the optimal c-bet strategy for BTN?

explanation.short: Small (33%) high frequency. Two-tone adds some draw equity but BTN still dominates with range adv + nut adv.
explanation.rangeLogic: Same dynamics as A-K-5 rainbow, with the addition that some BB combos pick up flush draws (e.g., QdJd, QdTd). This slightly equalizes equity but doesn't flip range advantage. BTN still bets ~75% of range.
explanation.nutLogic: BTN has nut flush draw combos (AdXd in opening range — though many AdKd type combos are now blockers to BB's flushes). Nut advantage stays with BTN.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) is best because (1) BTN has range advantage so betting most of range is +EV, (2) the small size keeps the pot manageable on a board where flush draws can complicate big-pot decisions, (3) it extracts thin value from BB's many marginal calls.
explanation.commonMistake: Going polar big on this board to 'charge the flush draw' loses EV — small high-freq value bets accomplish denial AND value extraction simultaneously, while big bets fold out the marginal calls that small bets get.

question.prompt: On Qh 9d 6s (semi-connected, rainbow), what's the optimal c-bet strategy?

explanation.short: Mixed small / check (~50/50). Modest range advantage with shared nut advantage = balanced strategy.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN has overpairs (TT-AA) and Q-x (KQ, AQ) for top pair. BB has 99, 66, sets, plus T8s, 87s, 76s, 65s for two-pair / draw equity. Range advantage tips slightly to BTN but not as dominantly as on A-high or K-high dry.
explanation.nutLogic: Sets are roughly even. Top pair (Q-x) leans BTN. Straight combos (87, T8) lean BB. Net nut advantage is roughly neutral.
explanation.sizingLogic: Mixed strategy: small bet with stronger Q-x and overpairs for value, check with weaker overpairs (88-TT) and air. Polar big is wrong because BTN's nut advantage isn't strong enough to support a bluff-heavy big sizing.
explanation.commonMistake: Defaulting to 'small high freq' on every board where BTN has range adv. On semi-wet boards like this, mixing in checks protects the checking range and avoids over-betting medium hands into the caller's many sets and draws.

question.prompt: On Th 8h 3h (monotone), who has nut advantage?

explanation.short: BTN — slight nut edge from holding more A-x and K-x suited Broadways.
explanation.rangeLogic: Range advantage on monotone boards is roughly neutral here — BB's range has many low/mid hearts (87s, 76s, 97s ) but BTN's wider range still picks up many flush combos.
explanation.nutLogic: The nut flush is A-X — BTN's range (A2s-AKs all suited) contains all suited Aces, and many of those are with hearts. BB's flatting range has fewer A-X combos because BB 3-bets ATs+, AJs+ at high frequency. BTN holds the nut flush (A-X) more often. Second-nut flush (K-X) similarly favors BTN. Net nut advantage = BTN, but moderate not crushing.
explanation.commonMistake: 'Monotone always means caller advantage' is wrong. On low monotone (5h4h2h), caller has more — but on T-high or higher monotone, the player with more high-suited hands (usually the raiser) holds nut advantage.

question.prompt: On 8s 7s 5s (monotone, low connected), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Caller (BB) crushes this board. BB has more low pairs, more 5x / 7x / 8x holdings, and more flush combos.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB's calling range contains many small suited connectors and one-gappers (87s, 76s, 65s, 54s, 86s, 75s) — many of which include the spade for a flush. BB also has 22-77 for sets. BTN's range is wider but heavily favors broadway and high cards that don't connect with this board.
explanation.nutLogic: Made flushes — BB's range contains low-spade flush combos (e.g., 96s , J4s if in flatting range). Straight combos (96, 64, 69s etc.) more frequent in BB. Nut flush A-X is roughly even since BB's A-X frequency is meaningful (A2s-A9s in BB, A-x). On low monotone, the calling range really does eclipse the raiser.
explanation.commonMistake: BTN players who 'always c-bet small' on monotone get punished here. Solver: BTN c-bets ~15 — 25% of range max, often as a small block bet with overpairs.

question.prompt: On Jh Th 4d (two-tone, semi-connected), how dynamic is this board?

explanation.short: Dynamic (3). Many straight draws (KQ, Q9, 98, 87) and an active flush draw.
explanation.commonMistake: Underrating two-tone semi-connected boards. The flush draw alone makes any board at least dynamic. Add the open-enders and gutshots to KQ, Q9, 98, 87, Q8 and you have constant equity churn.

question.prompt: On Qd 9c 4h (semi-dry rainbow), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN — moderate edge. Q-x (KQ, AQ, QJ) and overpairs (KK, AA) carry the range.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN opens KQ, AQ, QJs at high frequency. BB's calling range has QJs (some), JT, T9, QTs sparingly, but BB 3-bets KQ, AQ heavily. BTN's range is denser in top-pair Q-x and overpairs. BB has more 99 sets and small connector equity (98s for two pair, 8-x for second pair).
explanation.nutLogic: Sets QQ vs 99 vs 44 — QQ favors BTN (BTN opens at full frequency, BB sometimes 3-bets QQ). 99 even. 44 slight BB lean. Top pair Q-x clearly BTN. Net nut advantage modestly BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Some assume neutrality because of '9 in BB's range' — but BTN's overpair density and Q-x density outweighs the second-pair argument.

question.prompt: On Ah Jh 3s (two-tone), what sizing family is best for BTN?

explanation.short: Small high-freq (range_small). Two-tone adds some draw equity but A-high range advantage stays huge.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN dominates A-x range hugely. Two-tone slightly equalizes via flush draws (BB picks up QTs , KTs etc.) but range advantage stays clearly BTN.
explanation.nutLogic: BTN holds A-X for nut flush draws. Made flush is impossible on flop. Top of range still dominates BTN.
explanation.sizingLogic: Range_small (33%) is best because BTN can bet the entire range profitably. Polar big (75%) is acceptable as a secondary line for the very polar combos (sets + nut bluffs) but isn't the main strategy. Check-heavy bleeds EV.
explanation.commonMistake: Going too polar 'because there's a flush draw' loses EV — small bets with nearly the entire range still works on A-high two-tone.

question.prompt: On 9h 8c 7d (highly connected), what's the best c-bet strategy for BTN?

explanation.short: Check heavy. BB has range advantage and many made hands; betting most of range bleeds EV.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB's calling range has many T9s, 98s, 87s, 76s, J9s, T8s, plus 99, 88, 77, 66 etc. Many made straights, two-pair, sets. BTN's overpairs (TT-AA) are now medium hands at best.
explanation.nutLogic: Made straights live. Sets concentrated in BB. BTN's nut hands (sets of 9, 8, 7, made straight with JT) exist but at lower combo count than BB's nut hands.
explanation.sizingLogic: Check ~70%+ of range with everything except the strongest hands and selected polar bluffs (e.g., AQ with backdoor straight + overcards). When betting, use big sizing (~75%) polarized.
explanation.commonMistake: Range-c-betting small here is a critical leak — BTN gets check-raised constantly by BB's many made hands and draws, and the small bet doesn't fold out enough equity.

question.prompt: On Kh Jc 8s (semi-connected, rainbow), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN — moderate edge from K-x and J-x density.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN opens KJ, KQ, KT, AK, KJs+ at high frequency. BB calls some KT, KJ but 3-bets KQ/KJ often. BTN dominates K-x. J-x is closer (BB has JT, J9 etc.) but BTN still has more KJ, AJ, QJ.
explanation.nutLogic: Sets KK, JJ, 88 — KK heavily BTN, JJ split (some 3-bet), 88 slightly BB. Two pair KJ heavily BTN. Top pair K leaning BTN. Top of range BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Underrating K-high broadway boards. Even when there's some connectedness, the broadway-heavy nature still favors BTN.

question.prompt: On Tc 9d 2s (rainbow, T9 connected), how dynamic is the board?

explanation.short: Semi-static (2). T9 is connected but no flush draw and 2 is disconnected — limited equity swings.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating any two-card connection as 'dynamic' overestimates equity churn. T-9 is connected but the 2 doesn't extend the structure, and rainbow eliminates flush dynamics.

question.prompt: On 6c 5c 4s, what sizing family is best for BTN?

explanation.short: Check heavy. BB owns this board on every dimension.
explanation.rangeLogic: BB has all the connectors (76, 65, 54, 43, 87, 86), all the small pairs (44, 55, 66, 33, 22), and many flush-draw combos. BTN's overpairs are weak; BTN's broadway hands are mostly air.
explanation.nutLogic: Made straights and sets concentrated in BB. The 'top' of BTN's range here (AA, KK) often has under 50% equity vs BB's full continuing range.
explanation.sizingLogic: Check ~65 — 75% of range. When betting, polarize big with sets/straights and selected high-equity bluffs (e.g., AcQc combo draw). Small bets here get raised mercilessly.
explanation.commonMistake: Critical leak: range-c-betting small. The BB will check-raise relentlessly and BTN can't continue with most of the range. Polar big with low frequency is the only profitable bet line.

question.prompt: On 7d 7s 3c (paired low), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Split / mostly neutral. BTN has overpairs, BB has more 7-x and small pairs — they roughly balance.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN's overpair density (88-AA) is real but BB's 7-x combos (97s, 87s, 76s, 75s, 74s, 73s rare) and 33 sets (BTN's 33 mostly opens too — even split) keep things close. BB has slightly more underpairs (22, 33, 44, 55, 66) which all have meaningful showdown value but trail 88+.
explanation.nutLogic: Trips-7 require holding a 7 (77 is impossible — both 7s are on the board). Both ranges contain 7-x suited combos (87s, 76s, 97s, A7s, K7s) at similar rates; BB's wider flatting range gives a small edge on low 7-x density. Full house (33 + 7-x kicker) is rare in both ranges since pocket 33 is sparse and 3-x without a 7 doesn't make a boat. Overpairs (88-AA) lean BTN at the very top because BB 3-bets QQ+ heavily — BB's overpair density caps near JJ. Net nut advantage is roughly neutral with a slight BTN edge from premium overpairs.
explanation.commonMistake: Overweighting overpairs as 'range advantage' on paired low boards. Underpairs and trips combos balance things significantly. The texture supports a c-bet because BTN can fold equity from BB's many missed broadways, but the underlying range advantage is split.

question.prompt: BTN with AsKc on Ah Kd 5c. Action?

explanation.short: Bet 33%. Top two with the entire range bet — small high-freq value plus protection.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN bets the entire range small here. AK is value plus blocker — by holding both A and K, hero blocks BB's nutted A-x and K-x continues, so the bet folds out air (which is what BB has most often) and gets called by worse A-x and K-x.
explanation.handLogic: Top two is too strong to check — protection from runner-runner draws and value from K-x marginals is essential. The hand benefits from getting 3 streets of value when a low or rag turns.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) is best because BTN's whole range bets small. Big (75%) is acceptable as part of the polar mix but small is preferred — small extracts value from K-x and pocket pairs that fold to big.
explanation.commonMistake: Slowplaying top two on a dry board because 'I'm so strong.' This loses value — there's almost nothing for opponent to outdraw, but plenty of K-x and pocket pairs that pay off.
explanation.takeaway: Top two pair on dry A-high -> bet small high frequency for value.

question.prompt: BTN with QsQd on Ah Kd 5c. Action?

explanation.short: Bet 33%. Mostly bet for thin value + denial; checking is acceptable as part of the mix.
explanation.rangeLogic: On Ah Kd 5c — the GTO bet-frequency is high (~75%+). QQ wants to bet because it has equity vs many BB calls (bluff-catching with K-x high might call) and gains from denying equity to BB's overcards (J-x, T-x with backdoors).
explanation.handLogic: QQ here is not an overpair — it's an underpair to top two of board. But it's still ahead of BB's many K-x bluff-catchers (BB rarely has K-x given preflop; more often pocket pairs 22-JJ that QQ beats and overcards that miss). Check has merit if you want to keep weaker hands in for value, but most of the time bet small.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) keeps the pot manageable when BB has K-x or A-x. Bet 75% is wrong because it folds out the J-x, T-x, low pairs that QQ beats and only gets called by what beats it.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking QQ 'because I'm scared of A-x' is a common leak. BB's range is mostly missed broadways and small pairs — QQ bets small for value and protection.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair on dry A-high -> small bet for thin value; worse pairs and ace-light call.

question.prompt: BTN with 7h6h on Ah Kd 5c. Action?

explanation.short: Bet 33%. In a small high-freq strategy, bluff with backdoor equity is part of the bet range.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN bets ~75% of range small here. 76s has backdoor straight (with 4 or 8 to come) and backdoor flush (with hearts) — enough equity-when-called to bet for fold equity now.
explanation.handLogic: 76s is air on the flop but has multiple backdoor draws, making it a clear small-bluff candidate within the small high-freq strategy. Air without backdoors checks; air with backdoors bets.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) is the strategy. Big (75%) is wrong — bigger bluffs lose more when called and don't extract more fold equity than 33% on this dry board.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking pure air 'because I have nothing.' If BTN bets the value range small, BTN must also bluff small with the backdoor combos to balance — otherwise the value range is too transparent.
explanation.takeaway: Air with bdfd + bdsd on dry A-high -> small range stab as part of high-frequency strategy.

question.prompt: BTN with Td9d on Ah Kd 5c. Action?

explanation.short: Bet 33%. T9s with backdoor flush + backdoor straight + two overcards is a clear small-bet bluff.
explanation.handLogic: T9 of diamonds: backdoor flush (need 2 diamonds), backdoor straight (need J + Q or J + 8 to come), and Q/J overcards that can pair for showdown value. This is one of the best bluff candidates in BTN's range — multiple backdoor equity sources.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) gets folds from BB's air (J-x, Q-x, T-x air without backdoors) and keeps pot small for backdoor realization. Big (75%) folds out the same hands but commits more chips to a bluff — net negative.
explanation.commonMistake: Same as 76s — checking 'because I don't have a pair.' The backdoor density makes this a clear bet.
explanation.takeaway: Air with backdoors on dry A-high -> small range stab.

question.prompt: BTN with QcJc on Kh Td 2s. Action?

explanation.short: Bet 33%. Open-ended straight draw + two overcards = clear semi-bluff in a small high-freq strategy.
explanation.handLogic: QJ: open-ended (any A or 9 makes a straight) + Q/J overcards for pair showdown value. Strong semi-bluff candidate. Equity vs BB's continue range is high enough that even when called the hand has playability on most turns.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) is correct — same as the value range. Big bets on dry K-high are a leak.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating 'open-ended + overcards' as 'big bet semi-bluff' — on dry K-high, small high-freq is the strategy. Save big bets for dynamic boards where you actually need to charge equity.
explanation.takeaway: OESD + 2 broadway overcards on K-high -> small range bet as semi-bluff.

question.prompt: BTN with AhAs on 8h 7c 6s. Action?

explanation.short: Mostly check (~55%); bet big as part of polar mix (~40%). Small bet is wrong on this board.
explanation.rangeLogic: On 876, BTN's range is checking ~70%+. BB has too many made hands and draws to range-bet small profitably.
explanation.handLogic: AA is an overpair but not nutted — BB has straights, sets, two pair already. Checking protects the checking range (mixing AA in with weaker hands) and lets AA call multiple streets without bloating the pot OOP for BB's strong hands. Big bet (75%) is part of a polar protection line — denies equity to draws.
explanation.sizingLogic: If betting, go big (75%). Small (33%) is wrong because (a) it doesn't fold out enough draws, (b) it gets check-raised by BB's many made hands, and (c) it commits chips to a marginal-now-medium hand.
explanation.commonMistake: Range-c-betting AA small on every board. On dynamic low-connected boards, AA wants to either check for pot-control + induce or bet big for protection — the small bet is the worst of both worlds.
explanation.takeaway: AA on 8-7-6 (low connected wet) -> check; many sets/straights in BB range.

question.prompt: BTN with QcQs on Jh Ts 9c — (also a gutshot to K). Action?

explanation.short: Mix of check (~50%) and bet big (~40%). QQ is overpair + gutshot but BB has too much.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN checks ~50 — 60% on JT9 connected. QQ is in the mixing region — sometimes check for pot-control vs BB's straights (KQ, Q8) and two pair (JT, J9, T9), sometimes bet big for protection and value vs draws and worse pairs.
explanation.handLogic: QQ on JT9 has reasonable equity: ahead of one pair, behind two pair / sets / straights. Has gutshot to K for nut straight. Either line (check or bet big) plays the hand reasonably.
explanation.sizingLogic: If betting, big (75%) — denies equity to gutshots and underpairs while still extracting value from worse one-pair hands. Small (33%) is wrong because it doesn't fold out draws and gets raised.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-betting overpairs on connected boards. QQ is decent but not a clear bet; the connected nature and BB's range density means caution and big sizing-or-check is correct.
explanation.takeaway: QQ on J-T-9 (BB-favored) -> check; overpair is bluff-catcher despite OESD.

question.prompt: BTN with KhQh on Ah Jh 3s — (FD + gutshot + overcard). Action?

explanation.short: Bet — small or big both fine. Combo draw with overcard and nut flush draw is a premium semi-bluff.
explanation.rangeLogic: On Ah Jh 3s — BTN bets ~75% small. KQ of hearts: nut flush draw (best possible flush), gutshot to T (for Broadway straight), overcard to K. Equity vs continue range high; folds equity is meaningful.
explanation.handLogic: KhQh is one of the best semi-bluffing combos here — when called, has 12+ outs (9 flush outs, 3 K-high outs, sometimes Ts for straight). Both small and big bet sizing work.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (33%) plays the small high-freq strategy. Big (75%) is part of the polar mix — KhQh appears in both because of its raw equity. Check is wrong: you have a clear betting hand with great equity.
explanation.commonMistake: Slowplaying combo draws. Combo draws want to bet — they have fold equity AND hand-improving equity, both of which you forfeit by checking.
explanation.takeaway: Combo draw (K-FD + gutshot) on two-tone -> small/big bet for equity realization.

question.prompt: BTN with KhKs on 6c 5c 4s. Action?

explanation.short: Check often (~55%); bet big as protection sometimes (~40%). Small bet is the worst option.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN checks ~65 — 75% of range here. KK is in the mixing zone — strong but vulnerable.
explanation.handLogic: KK on 654 has decent equity: ahead of all underpairs and most one-pair, behind made straights (87, 76, 73 unlikely), behind sets (66, 55, 44 — all in BB more often). Many turn cards (3, 7, 8, club for flush) are bad. Checking realizes equity by getting to showdown cheaper; betting big denies draws and gets value from worse pairs and FDs.
explanation.sizingLogic: Big (75%) for protection if betting — accomplish the fold-equity goal. Small (33%) gets check-raised constantly here and doesn't deny enough equity. Small bet on this board is a meaningful leak.
explanation.commonMistake: 'Overpair = always bet small' — wrong on dynamic wet boards. KK is good but not great on 654, and the board demands either check or big, never small.
explanation.takeaway: KK on 6-5-4 -> check; many straights and sets in BB range.

explanation.rangeLogic: Low dry boards (e.g., 9-6-2 rainbow) favor the player with overpairs and high cards. BTN open has TT-AA at full frequency, plus broadway floats; BB 3-bets the same pairs, leaving BTN with overpair advantage.
explanation.commonMistake: Going polar (75%+ pot) on low dry rainbow is a leak - there's nothing to charge, and small wins more in the long run.

question.prompt: On 8c 5h 2d, who has nut advantage?

explanation.short: BTN. Top of range (overpairs, top-kicker, sets) lives in the raiser preflop range.
explanation.nutLogic: BTN holds AA-TT and AKo/AQo as overpairs and overcards; BB has small pairs but they're often blocked by the board (e.g., 22-66 with 6-x on board).
explanation.commonMistake: Going polar (75%+ pot) on low dry rainbow is a leak - there's nothing to charge, and small wins more in the long run.

question.prompt: On Ah Ad 7c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN. Range tilts toward the raiser on this board class.
explanation.rangeLogic: BTN open is K/Q/J/A-x heavy, so BTN has more trip combos (e.g., AK/AJ on AAx; KQ/KJ on KKx). BB 3-bets these dominant kickers preflop, further increasing BTN's trips advantage.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking back too often on paired high boards costs value - most of BTN's range is ahead of BB's calling range.

question.prompt: On Th Td 4c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Roughly neutral - neither range hits this board significantly harder.
explanation.rangeLogic: Paired middle boards (T-T-x, 9-9-x, 8-8-x) shift slightly toward BB because BB flats more 8x / 9x / Tx suited combos that hit trips. BTN still has overpair equity, but the trips density edge belongs to BB.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-betting paired boards regardless of rank is a leak - middle pairs flip the trips density.

question.prompt: On Th 9d 8c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), who has range advantage?

question.prompt: On As 9s 4d (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BTN. Range tilts toward the raiser on this board class.
explanation.rangeLogic: A-high or K-high two-tone disconnected board. BTN open carries far more A-x and K-x combos than BB's preflop call range, and BB 3-bets dominant top-pair combos preflop. Range advantage clearly BTN.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating two-tone exactly like rainbow (range-small always) misses value vs. BB's flush draws; check-heavy concedes the board to BB unnecessarily.

explanation.sizingLogic: Mixed small/check: bet ~33-50% with ~60-70% frequency. Slightly more polarized than rainbow because BB has flush draws to charge. Range_small is also defensible on the dryer end (A-x-x with low x).
explanation.commonMistake: Treating two-tone exactly like rainbow (range-small always) misses value vs. BB's flush draws; check-heavy concedes the board to BB unnecessarily.

question.prompt: On Qh Jh 4c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: Roughly neutral - neither range hits this board significantly harder.
explanation.rangeLogic: Q/J/T-high two-tone disconnected board. BTN has overpairs and high cards but BB's flatting range is dense in suited middle hands (T9s, JTs, QJs, 87s, etc.) that connect with this board. Range advantage close to neutral; not a clear BTN spot.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto range-small on Q/J/T two-tone is a leak; this is not an A/K-high board.

explanation.short: Sizing family: mixed_small_check. Mixed small/check: ~50/50 between small bet and check. Polar big is wasteful her...

question.prompt: On 9c 7c 5d (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), who has range advantage?

explanation.short: BB. Range tilts toward the caller - connectors and small pairs hit harder.
explanation.rangeLogic: Low connected two-tone board (8-7-x suited, 9-8-x suited, T-9-x with low x and two of a suit). BB's flatting range is full of suited connectors (76s, 87s, 98s, T9s, 65s, 54s) that hit top pair, two-pair, sets, straights, AND flush draws on the suit. BTN's overpairs are bluff-catchers.
explanation.commonMistake: C-betting low connected two-tone with range-small is one of the biggest postflop leaks in MTT play. Check this board the majority of the time.

question.prompt: With AhKh on As 8d 3h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Top pair top kicker on a dry A-high board. Bet small with high frequency for value.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN has clear range and nut advantage on A-high dry rainbow boards. Small high-frequency c-bets dominate.
explanation.handLogic: AK is the top of BTN's range here. It wants to bet for value against worse Ax, K-high floats, and pocket pairs that defend. Slow-playing risks giving up streets where BB folds out worse hands cheaply.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) extracts value from a wide thin-calling range without folding out the underpair / K-high portion. Big sizing chases away the marginal hands AK actually beats.
explanation.commonMistake: Overbetting with AK on dry A-high boards. The pot doesn't need to grow this fast and worse hands fold to big bets. Bet small with high frequency.
explanation.takeaway: Strong top pair on dry A-high — bet small with frequency.

question.prompt: With 7c6c on As 8d 3h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with backdoors on dry A-high. Range-stab small as part of high-frequency c-bet.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN range-bets dry A-high boards at high frequency. Air hands with backdoor equity carry the bluff frequency.
explanation.handLogic: 76 has zero showdown value but enough backdoor equity (BDFD + BDSD with the 8 on board) to continue as a bluff if called. Checking gives up the stab equity for nothing.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) keeps the cost of the bluff low while still folding out a meaningful portion of BB's air and weak underpairs. Big sizing burns chips on a hand with no realisation behind it.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting air on a dry A-high board. The board class wants small high-frequency, not polar-big.
explanation.takeaway: Dry A-high with air + backdoors — bet small as part of the range-stab.

question.prompt: With 9d9s on As 8d 3h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Medium pocket pair on A-high. Check for pot control — Ax calls, weak hands fold.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN's medium pairs (TT down to 22 below the ace) realise more equity by checking back on A-high boards than by betting.
explanation.handLogic: 99 beats only worse pocket pairs and missed broadways. Betting makes weaker hands fold (we get nothing) and stronger hands (Ax) call (we lose). Checking gets us to showdown cheaply.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-c-betting because we have a pair. Medium pairs on A-high should check back the majority of the time — pot control is the whole point.
explanation.takeaway: Mid pocket pair on A-high — check for pot control.

question.prompt: With QcJh on As 8d 3h, BTN's recommended action is to bet small as part of the range-stab. What is the main reason?

choices.label: Range advantage — bet most of the range cheaply
Value — get worse hands to call
Give up — no equity, save chips

explanation.short: Range stab — BTN's range advantage makes small high-frequency betting +EV even with no-equity hands.
explanation.rangeContext: Dry A-high boards favour BTN's whole range. The frame is range betting, not hand-by-hand bluffing.
explanation.handLogic: QJ has no made hand and no real draw, but BTN bets it as part of the range strategy: small sizing, high frequency, with the threat of further pressure on later streets.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling this a 'bluff' is technically correct but misses the strategic frame. The right mental model is range advantage, not isolated bluffing.
explanation.takeaway: On range-advantaged boards, the reason to bet air is range, not bluff.

question.prompt: With KsQc on Kh 9c 4s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Top pair good kicker on K-high. Bet small for value and protection.
explanation.rangeContext: K-high rainbow boards are range-advantaged for BTN; small high-frequency betting is the dominant strategy.
explanation.handLogic: KQ is one of the strongest top pairs we routinely have here (AK 3-bet preflop a lot, so KQ becomes a top of our value range). Bet small to get value from worse Kx, second pairs, and protect against the few overcards (A only) that can outdraw us.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) extracts the most calls from the long tail of bluff-catchers BB defends. Big sizing folds out worse hands and inflates the pot needlessly.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating KQ as worth a polar overbet. On rainbow K-high, range bet small.
explanation.takeaway: Top pair good kicker on dry K-high — bet small for value.

question.prompt: With JdTd on Kh 9c 4s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Overcard + gutshot + backdoor on K-high. Semi-bluff with the small range bet.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN's strategy is range bet small on dry K-high; JT carries a useful equity bundle (J overcard + Q gutshot + backdoor diamonds).
explanation.handLogic: JT has fold equity from the J overcard, plus 4 outs to a straight (Q), plus a backdoor flush draw. That's enough to bet as a semi-bluff and continue against most lines.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small bet keeps the cost of the bluff low and folds out air / weak underpairs. Big bet over-charges for a hand that doesn't have made-hand value.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting JT for fold equity. The board doesn't support a polar strategy yet — small range bet is correct.
explanation.takeaway: Overcard + gutshot + BDFD — small range bet as semi-bluff.

question.prompt: With 7h7c on Kh 9c 4s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Small underpair below 9. Check for pot control and showdown.
explanation.rangeContext: Below the second board card, 77 is a weak-showdown hand that wants to see the river cheaply.
explanation.handLogic: 77 beats only smaller pairs and pure air. Betting folds out hands we beat and gets called by the K-x and 9-x range that beats us. Check, plan to fold to one bet on most run-outs.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-c-betting every pocket pair to 'protect'. Below the second board card, the pair is too weak to bet for protection — you're protecting against hands that already beat you.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair below the second board card — check, accept showdown value.

choices.label: Range advantage — bet most of the range cheaply
Value — get worse hands to call

Give up — no equity, save chips

explanation.short: Range stab on K-high; the A blocker is a small additional reason but not the primary one.
explanation.rangeContext: The strategic frame on K-high dry boards is the same as A-high dry: BTN range bets small at high frequency.
explanation.handLogic: AQ has 6 outs to a pair plus a small backdoor bundle. The decision to bet is driven by range advantage, not by the A blocker — the A blocker is a secondary reason that confirms the bet but isn't the primary engine.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling AQ a 'value bet' here. AQ doesn't beat much yet; it's an equity-realisation + range-stab hand.
explanation.takeaway: On range-advantaged boards, the primary reason to bet AQ is range, not value.

question.prompt: With JhJc on 8s 7d 5h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Overpair on a low-connected wet board where BB has range and nut advantage. Check.
explanation.rangeContext: 8-7-5 is the textbook check-heavy board for BTN. BB's flatting range is loaded with two-pair, sets, and straight combos; BTN's overpairs are bluff-catchers more than value hands.
explanation.handLogic: JJ on 8-7-5 is ahead of one pair but behind every two-pair, set, and made straight in BB's range. Betting bloats the pot for a hand that gets check-raised by everything that beats us. Check, see a turn, re-evaluate.
explanation.commonMistake: Betting big with overpairs 'for protection' on low-connected boards. This is one of the textbook critical leaks Module 2 is designed to flag — protection bets on these boards lose money against the hands that beat you.
explanation.takeaway: Overpair on 8-7-5-style boards — check, do not 'protect'.

question.prompt: With AhQc on 8s 7d 5h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Two overcards no equity on a board BB owns. Check, realise equity, plan to fold most turns.
explanation.rangeContext: Low connected boards favour BB; BTN should check the air portion of the range (no two-pair-plus, no real draw).
explanation.handLogic: AQ has 6 outs to top pair but the texture means even an A or Q doesn't make the best hand often. Bluffing here gets called or raised by the dense BB range — checking lets us realise equity without paying.
explanation.commonMistake: Stabbing with AQ because 'BTN should c-bet'. Wrong board class — over-c-betting low connected boards is a textbook leak.
explanation.takeaway: Air on 8-7-5-check, see if a free turn helps.

question.prompt: With 9c6c on 8s 7d 5h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Made straight (9-8-7-6-5). Bet big to charge worse made hands and balance the polar range.
explanation.rangeContext: On 8-7-5, BTN's value bets are concentrated in straights, sets, and two pair. Polar big sizing extracts maximum from BB's overpair / two pair calls.
explanation.handLogic: 96 made the second-nut straight. We have BB's two-pair / set range crushed and they will pay off a big bet. Checking back leaves money on the table.
explanation.sizingLogic: Big (~75-100%) maximises value from sets and overpairs that don't fold and balances our future polar-bluff frequency on this board.
explanation.commonMistake: Slow-playing the made straight. On wet textures, betting big extracts value from worse hands that will improve to second-best on the turn.
explanation.takeaway: Made straight on wet low connected — bet big polar.

question.prompt: With KsKd on 8s 7d 5h, BTN's recommended action is to check. What is the main reason?

choices.label: Pot control — overpair is a bluff-catcher here
Protection — bet big to deny equity
Value — get worse hands to call
Give up — fold to all pressure

explanation.short: KK is a bluff-catcher on 8-7-5. Check to keep the pot small, not to 'protect'.
explanation.rangeContext: BB's range hits this board hard. KK is ahead of single pairs and bluffs but behind two-pair, sets, and straights.
explanation.handLogic: Checking achieves pot control — we still beat BB's bluffs and underpairs but avoid bloating against the hands that beat us. The 'protection' frame is wrong here: we're not protecting equity, we're avoiding a check-raise.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling this a 'protection' check or, worse, making it a 'protection bet'. The classic critical leak is betting overpairs big on low connected boards in the name of protection.
explanation.takeaway: Overpair on low-connected wet — check is pot control, not protection.

question.prompt: With AhKc on Th 6h 2c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Two overcards + ace-blocker on two-tone T-high. Bet small as part of the range-stab; the Ah blocker is supporting evidence, not a polar-bluff trigger.
explanation.rangeContext: Two-tone T-high boards favour BTN slightly; modern strategy is small high-frequency betting with the air range that holds backdoor equity or relevant blockers. AhKc fits that air-with-blockers slot — it is NOT a real flush draw because hero has only 1 heart + 2 board hearts = 3 hearts (a backdoor draw, not a 4-card flush draw).
explanation.handLogic: AhKc has 6 outs to top pair (3 each for A and K) plus a backdoor flush draw and ace-blocker effect. That's enough equity + leverage to bet, but it is NOT a polar-bluff candidate — overestimating the equity by treating it as a real NFD is the mistake to avoid.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) keeps the bet consistent with the range-stab strategy on T-high two-tone. Big sizing over-bluffs a hand that doesn't have made-hand or real-draw equity.
explanation.commonMistake: Confusing 'backdoor flush draw + ace blocker' with 'real nut flush draw'. With only 3 hearts in the hand+board, the flush is two cards away, not one. Polar-overbetting this hand leaks chips against BB's two-pair / set / flush-draw continuing range.
explanation.takeaway: Air with ace-blocker + backdoors on two-tone — small range stab, not polar bluff.

question.prompt: With Tc8s on Th 6h 2c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Top pair with a weak kicker on a flush-draw-rich board. Bet small for thin value + protection.
explanation.rangeContext: On two-tone T-high boards, BTN should still bet for value with top pairs but at the smaller sizing because BB's range contains many flush draws that won't fold to small.
explanation.handLogic: T8 is top pair with kicker concerns. We want value from BB's underpairs and 9-x and protection against the heart draws. Small accomplishes both without escalating the pot for a marginal hand.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) keeps draws calling at correct price for our equity defence and lets us fold without much loss to a check-raise. Big sizing inflates the pot for a hand that doesn't beat much when called heavily.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar overbetting weak top pair. Small high-frequency is the right size on two-tone with thin value hands.
explanation.takeaway: Weak top pair on two-tone — small for thin value + protection.

question.prompt: With 4d4c on Th 6h 2c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Small underpair on a flush-draw board. Check, take cheap showdown when you can.
explanation.rangeContext: 44 is below most of BB's calling range and has no draw equity. The two-tone texture also means flushing turns / rivers will price us out anyway.
explanation.handLogic: Betting folds out hands we beat (worse underpairs, missed broadways) and gets called by everything that beats us (Tx, 6x, flush draws). Check, plan to fold most turns.
explanation.commonMistake: Stabbing with small pocket pairs on two-tone boards 'because we have something'. The pair is too weak to bet, and the flush draw board makes our equity even thinner.
explanation.takeaway: Small underpair on two-tone — check, accept the showdown.

question.prompt: With 9h8h on Th 6h 2c, BTN's recommended action is to bet (commonly bet big). What is the main reason?

choices.label: Equity realization — strong draw wants to bet for fold equity + value when it hits
Value — get worse hands to call
Thin value — extract from worse marginal hands
Blocker pressure — block opponent's strong calls

explanation.short: Combo draw with raw equity. Bet big to combine fold equity with realisation when the draw hits.
explanation.rangeContext: Combo draws are the prime semi-bluff candidates on two-tone wet boards. Strategic frame is equity realisation, not value.
explanation.handLogic: 9h8h has flush draw + gutshot (7 makes 6-7-8-9-10) plus 2 pair outs. Betting now gives us fold equity AND we still have 12+ outs when called.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling this a value bet. We have no made hand — the bet is a semi-bluff with equity realisation as the strategic engine.
explanation.takeaway: Combo draws bet for equity realisation, not value.

question.prompt: With QhQc on Kc Kd 7s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: QQ on paired K-high. Bet small for thin value — but checking is also fully GTO, since QQ is in the in-between region.
explanation.rangeContext: Paired K-high boards favour BTN heavily. Small high-frequency c-bets work, and QQ is one of the hands that mixes between bet_small and check in modern solver outputs.
explanation.handLogic: QQ is ahead of TT, JJ, 99, 88, and most 7-x; behind only KK, AA, AK, K-x trips. Betting small extracts value from the long bluff-catcher tail; checking realises showdown equity cheaply when BB does not pay off (BB's continuing range vs a small bet is mostly worse pairs and ace-high floats).
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) gets called by 88-JJ and ace-high floats. Check is GTO-defensible because QQ is too thin for big and the value tail vs small isn't huge.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar overbetting QQ. The right frame on paired K-high is small high-frequency, not polar big — and check is acceptable when balancing the checking range.
explanation.takeaway: QQ on paired K-high — small for thin value, with check fully acceptable.

scenarios.blockerNote: Ace blocker — BB rarely has AK in the calling range; A overcard adds equity outs

question.prompt: With AsQs on Kc Kd 7s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with A blocker on paired K-high. Range stab small — range advantage is the engine, blocker is supporting evidence.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN range bets paired K-high small at high frequency; AQ carries A blocker plus 6 pair outs as the bluff component, but the strategic frame is range advantage, not isolated blocker betting.
explanation.handLogic: AQ has no made hand but the A blocker reduces some of BB's strongest continuing combos and the A overcard gives us a turn equity bump. The primary reason to bet, however, is range advantage: BTN bets paired K-high small with a wide range and AQ slots into the bluff portion of that range strategy.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) is consistent with the value range we'd bet here (overpairs, trips). Big sizing isolates us against trips that never fold.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting AQ as a bluff. Wrong size — paired K-high is a small high-frequency board class.
explanation.takeaway: A-blocker air on paired K-high — small range stab.

question.prompt: With 6c6d on Kc Kd 7s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Small underpair below 7. Check, accept showdown — betting folds out worse and gets called by 7-x and trips.
explanation.rangeContext: On paired K-high, BTN bets small with a wide value+bluff range. Sub-7 underpairs are the showdown floor that prefers checking.
explanation.handLogic: 66 beats only 22-55 and pure air. Better to check, see a turn, fold to most pressure than to bet and either fold out worse or get called by 7-x trips and overpairs.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-c-betting any pocket pair on paired boards. Below the bottom unpaired card, the pair is a check-take-showdown hand.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair below the bottom card on paired board — check.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds Kh — blocks a key trips combo from BB

question.prompt: With AhKh on Kc Kd 7s, BTN holds trip Kings with the top kicker. The recommended strategy mixes between bet_small and check. When BTN checks back trip Ks as a deliberate trap, what is the main reason?

choices.label: Value (delayed) — induce future barrels / keep dominated hands in
Pot control — keep pot small with marginal value

explanation.short: Trip Ks + ace kicker. Checking is for delayed value — let BB barrel turn or give us a free card to extract more later.
explanation.rangeContext: On paired Kx, the strongest hands like AhKh mix between betting for thin range value and checking to balance the checking range and induce.
explanation.handLogic: We crush BB's calling range. Betting small extracts thin value; checking lets BB take a stab on the turn or makes it harder for them to fold middling pairs and ace-high floats. Both lines are correct — the check line aims at delayed value, not pot control.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling the check 'give up' or 'pot control'. With trips top kicker, we're trapping for value, not surrendering or controlling pot size.
explanation.takeaway: Strong made hand on paired board — checking is for value (trap), not for pot control.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds Ah on a monotone hearts board. Ah is the nut card — when the flush completes, hero has the nut flush. Hero has 1 heart + 3 board hearts = 4 hearts — real flush draw (need 1 more heart on turn or river). NOT a made flush.

question.prompt: With AhTd on Jh 8h 4h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Nut flush draw on monotone — bet small as part of the small-frequency strategy on monotone, or check to balance the checking range.
explanation.rangeContext: Monotone boards reward small sizings. The Ah is the nut card; hero has 4 hearts in hand+board (1 hero + 3 board) — real flush draw needing one more heart. Hero blocks BB's nut combos and has nut equity if the draw completes.
explanation.handLogic: AhTd has 9 outs to the nut flush, plus the Ah blocker effect on BB's continuing range. It's a strong semi-bluff candidate, but on monotone the dominant sizing is small (rather than polar big), because big chases away the one-pair / lower-FD calling range.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) extracts thin equity-denial value from one-pair-with-heart calls and lower flush draws while preserving fold equity. Big sizing is dominated on monotone — it only gets called by made flushes (we lose) and folds out the marginal calls.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting the nut flush draw on monotone, or worse, treating the Ah as if it already gave us a made nut flush (it doesn't — we have 4 of one suit, not 5).
explanation.takeaway: Nut flush DRAW on monotone (not made) — small bet keeps marginal calls in and preserves nut blocker pressure.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds Kh on monotone hearts — second-nut FLUSH DRAW (1 hero heart + 3 board hearts = 4 hearts — need 1 more heart). Kh blocker reduces some opponent K-flush combos; Q overcard adds 3 outs. NOT a made flush.

question.prompt: With KhQd on Jh 8h 4h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: K-flush DRAW (not made) on monotone. Mixed strategy — bet small at moderate frequency or check for equity realization.
explanation.rangeContext: On monotone J-high, BTN's strategy is check-heavy. K-FD is a second-nut FD with the Kh blocker — it has equity but is dominated by AhX nut FDs in BB's range. Modern solver outputs mix between small bet and check.
explanation.handLogic: KhQd has 9 outs to a K-high or higher flush, plus 3 outs to a Q-pair (some of which improve us to top pair). The Kh blocker reduces a small slice of BB's continuing range. But the hand is dominated when the flush completes — Ah-X always beats us — so betting big is wrong, and check is fully GTO.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) at moderate frequency is fine for the bet-portion of the mix, taking thin equity-denial value. Big is wrong because it inflates the pot when we get called by hands that mostly beat us (made flushes).
explanation.commonMistake: Treating Kh on monotone hearts as a 'made K-flush.' Hero has 4 hearts (1 hero + 3 board) — that's a flush DRAW, not a made flush. Need a 5th heart on turn or river.
explanation.takeaway: K-flush DRAW on monotone — mixed: small bet or check; bet_big is rarely correct.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds zero hearts — no flush blocker

question.prompt: With 9d9c on Jh 8h 4h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Underpair with zero hearts on monotone. Check, accept showdown — no flush, no blocker, no bet.
explanation.rangeContext: Without a heart, our equity to make the flush is 0. We're a pure bluff-catcher against any heart and a small favourite over air.
explanation.handLogic: 99 beats 22-77 with no heart and pure air. Betting only gets called by hands that beat us (any heart, J-x, sets). Check, fold to most pressure.
explanation.commonMistake: Stabbing with underpairs on monotone 'because we have a pair'. Without a flush blocker, the pair is too vulnerable.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair with no heart on monotone — check, don't bluff yourself.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero has 1 heart (6h) + 3 board hearts = 4 hearts — flush DRAW (not made). When the flush completes, the 5-card flush is at-best Jh-high (the highest heart in the 5-card combo), and any opponent heart higher than 6 dominates this draw. Plus a gutshot to 7 for 6-7-8-9 straight (or straight flush if 7h hits).

question.prompt: With 6h5c on Jh 8h 4h, BTN's recommended action is to check despite holding a flush draw plus a gutshot. What is the main reason?

choices.label: Give up — flush draw is reverse-dominated by every higher heart in BB's range
Pot control — keep pot small with marginal equity
Value — bet to get worse to call
Equity realization — bet to leverage fold equity

explanation.short: Low flush DRAW is reverse-dominated by every higher heart in BB's range — check to give up cheaply.
explanation.rangeContext: On monotone hearts, hero with only the 6h holds the lowest possible flush DRAW (not a made flush — hero has 4 hearts, needs 5). When the flush completes on turn or river, every higher heart in BB's range (7h-Ah) beats us.
explanation.handLogic: 6h5c has 9 outs to complete the flush, but most of those outs make a hand that LOSES to BB's higher heart draws or holdings. Plus the gutshot (7) adds 4 outs (3 if we don't double-count 7h). Net effective equity is much lower than a normal FD because of the reverse-domination problem. Betting bluffs into a stronger range; checking gives up cheaply.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating the 6h on monotone as a 'made low flush' (it isn't — it's a low FD), or betting the FD 'for fold equity' when most of the outs we hit are dominated. Worst of all: betting for 'value' — we have neither a made hand nor positive equity vs calling ranges.
explanation.takeaway: Reverse-dominated low flush draw on monotone — check / give up; betting for value is the trap to avoid.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds Ah on a two-tone hearts board (3 hearts total, BDFD only). Ah blocks a slice of BB's nut flush draws (no AhX) and gives a small overcard equity bump.

question.prompt: With AhTc on Kh 9h 4c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with A-blocker + bdfd on K-high two-tone. Small range stab uses the blocker.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN range-bets K-high two-tone at moderate frequency; A-blocker air fits the bluff portion of that strategy.
explanation.handLogic: AhTc has zero made-hand value but the Ah reduces BB's nut flush draw combos and the A overcard adds 3 outs. Range advantage on K-high two-tone supports a small stab; the blocker is supporting evidence, not the engine.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) is consistent with the value range we'd bet here. Big sizing chases away the marginal call range and burns chips with no made hand.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting A-blocker air on a two-tone board. The blocker reduces BB's strong hands but doesn't turn this into a value or polar bluff candidate.
explanation.takeaway: A-blocker air on K-high two-tone -> small range stab.

scenarios.blockerNote: A-blocker reduces BB's AK trips combos. Hero hearts (Ah, Jh) are off-suit relative to the club texture so no flush draw component.

question.prompt: With AhJh on Kc Kd 4c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with A-blocker on paired K. Small range stab — paired K is range-advantaged for BTN.
explanation.rangeContext: Paired K boards favour BTN heavily; small high-frequency c-bets dominate the strategy.
explanation.handLogic: AhJh has no made hand and no real draw, but the Ah blocks one of BB's few AK trips combos and the A overcard offers 3 outs. On paired K boards, BTN range bets small at high frequency — air with blockers fits the bluff portion of that strategy.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) is the standard sizing on paired K boards: extracts thin value from underpairs, gets folds from air, keeps the pot small.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar overbetting A-blocker air on paired K. Wrong size — paired K-high is small high-frequency.
explanation.takeaway: A-blocker air on paired K -> small range stab.

scenarios.blockerNote: AK with K-Q-J-(T)-(?) gutshot to T (4 outs to nut straight). K-blocker reduces BB's K-paired and KK combos. Plus 6 pair outs on A or K.

question.prompt: With AdKs on Qh Jh 8c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Gutshot to nut straight + 2 overcards + K-blocker on Q-high two-tone. Small semi-bluff with strong equity.
explanation.rangeContext: Q-J-8 two-tone is BB-favored at the top of range but BTN bets the air-with-equity portion small for fold equity + realisation.
explanation.handLogic: AdKs has 4 outs to the nut straight (T), 6 outs to top pair (A or K), plus the K-blocker effect on BB's K-paired and KK continuing range. Strong semi-bluff: bet for fold equity now and continue with equity if called.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) extracts maximum from BB's wide call-down range while keeping bluff cost low. Big sizing over-charges for a hand that wants to keep BB's air calling.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking the nut-straight gutshot + overcards + blocker. Combined equity is too high to give up.
explanation.takeaway: Nut-straight gutshot + overcards + K-blocker -> small semi-bluff.

scenarios.blockerNote: K-blocker reduces BB's AhKh and AdKh trips combos. Hero has no A so no trips for hero.

question.prompt: With KhQc on Ah Ad 8s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with K-blocker on paired A. Small range stab — BTN range bets paired Ax small with high freq.
explanation.rangeContext: Paired A boards heavily favour BTN; small high-frequency c-bets dominate.
explanation.handLogic: KhQc has no made hand and no draw, but the Kh blocks BB's strongest non-trip continuing hand (KK is unblocked but AK with Kh kicker is reduced). On paired A boards BTN range bets at very high frequency; air with K-blocker fits the bluff portion.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) is standard for paired Ax. Big sizing over-bluffs and isolates against trips that never fold.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting K-blocker air on paired A. The right frame is small high-frequency, not polar bluff.
explanation.takeaway: K-blocker air on paired A -> small range stab.

question.prompt: With KsQs on Kc 9d 4h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Top pair good kicker on dry K-high. Bet small for value with high frequency.
explanation.rangeContext: Dry K-high boards favour BTN clearly; small high-frequency betting is the dominant strategy.
explanation.handLogic: KQ is one of the strongest top pairs we routinely have here (AK 3-bets preflop a lot, removing K-x with the best kicker from BB's flatting range and from our cold-call range). Small bet extracts value from worse Kx, second pairs, and underpairs.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) is the value-maximising sizing on dry K-high. Big bet folds out worse hands and inflates the pot for a marginal protection benefit.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating KQ as polar overbet material. On dry K-high, bet small with high frequency.
explanation.takeaway: Top pair good kicker on dry K-high -> bet small for value.

scenarios.blockerNote: Bottom set blocks BB's natural Tx-trips combos.

question.prompt: With TcTd on Th 6s 2c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Set of T's on dry T-high. Bet small high frequency for value; bet big also acceptable polar.
explanation.rangeContext: Dry T-high low-disconnected boards favour BTN range; set is the top of value range and bets at high frequency.
explanation.handLogic: TT-set is near-nut on T-high low-disconnected boards. Small bet extracts thin value from a wide bluff-catcher tail (overpairs, broadway floats); big bet polarises but folds out the long tail. Both are GTO; small high-frequency dominates in modern outputs.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) extracts the most calls from underpairs and overcards. Big (~75%) polar is also fine when you want to charge BB's rare 6x or 2x trips.
explanation.commonMistake: Slow-playing the set on dry. Even on a dry texture, betting extracts more EV than checking back.
explanation.takeaway: Set on dry T-high -> bet small (or polar big); never check back.

scenarios.blockerNote: Heart bdfd irrelevant on rainbow board. Hero blocks JhTh straight-draw combo only minimally.

question.prompt: With KhQh on Ks Qc 8d (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Top two pair on K-Q-8. Bet small for value; bet big also fine to charge JT and broadway draws.
explanation.rangeContext: K-Q-8 is range-advantaged for BTN with broadway-heavy flatting range; top two is the top of value range.
explanation.handLogic: KhQh = top two pair, near-nut on K-Q-8 (loses only to KK, QQ, 88-set, AJ-straight on a turn-J). Bet for value while charging BB's gutshot/OESD draws (JT, JJ, T9 etc.). Small extracts maximum from BB's wide bluff-catcher range; big is also defensible to charge draws.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) maximises calls from one-pair K-x and Q-x. Big (~75%) is fine to charge JT and other straight draws on this slightly connected texture.
explanation.commonMistake: Slow-playing top two on a connected board. JT is a real draw for BB; checking gives free cards.
explanation.takeaway: Top two pair on K-Q-8 -> bet for value (small or big both fine).

question.prompt: With 8c7c on As 9c 3d (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with bdfd + bdsd on dry A-high. Small range stab as part of the high-frequency strategy.
explanation.rangeContext: Dry A-high boards favour BTN heavily; range betting small at high frequency dominates.
explanation.handLogic: 8c7c has no showdown value but carries bdfd (3 clubs total) and a backdoor straight to T (backdoor 5-6 or 6-T to make 5-6-7-8-9 or 7-8-9-T-J). Range advantage on dry A-high lets us stab small with this air, and the backdoor equity supports continuing on favourable turns.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) keeps the bluff cost low and folds out enough of BB's air and weak underpairs. Big sizing burns chips with no made hand.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting air on dry A-high. Wrong size — A-high dry is small high-frequency.
explanation.takeaway: Air with backdoors on dry A-high -> small range stab.

scenarios.blockerNote: Backdoor spade FD via KsQs (3 spades total).

question.prompt: With KsQs on As Td 5c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with KQ overcards + backdoor spade FD on dry A-high. Small range stab.
explanation.rangeContext: Dry A-high range bet small with high frequency; broadway air with backdoors fits the bluff portion.
explanation.handLogic: KsQs has no made hand but has 6 outs to top pair (K or Q), plus a backdoor spade flush draw (3 spades total). Range advantage on dry A-high supports a small stab; the equity bundle gives a comfortable continue on most turns.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) is consistent with the range strategy. Big sizing isolates against an A-x range that calls.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting KQ on dry A-high. Wrong size for the air range here.
explanation.takeaway: KQ overcards + bdfd on dry A-high -> small range stab.

question.prompt: With JdTh on Kc 9c 4d (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: JT gutshot + 2 overcards on K-high two-tone. Small range stab + semi-bluff.
explanation.rangeContext: K-high two-tone range bets at moderate frequency for BTN; air with broadway equity fits the bluff portion.
explanation.handLogic: JdTh has 4 outs to the nut straight (Q gives KQJT9), 6 outs to top pair (J or T), plus the air-equity bundle that lets us continue. Range advantage on K-high two-tone supports a small stab even without the gutshot.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) keeps the cost low and folds out air. Big bet over-prices a hand without made-hand value.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting JT for fold equity. Small range bet is the right size on K-high two-tone.
explanation.takeaway: JT gutshot + overcards on K-high two-tone -> small range stab + semi-bluff.

question.prompt: With 8c8s on Kh Td 4s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Mid pocket pair (between K and 4) on K-high. Check for pot control.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN's underpair-to-second-board-card hands prefer checking on K-high to retain showdown value.
explanation.handLogic: 88 is below T-pair and K-pair on this board — beats only smaller underpairs and pure air. Betting folds out worse and gets called by Tx, Kx, Jx that beat us. Check, see a turn, often fold to pressure.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-c-betting because we have a pair. Below the second board card the pair is too weak to bet for value or protection.
explanation.takeaway: Mid pocket pair on K-high -> check for pot control.

question.prompt: With JhJc on Qd Th 6s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Underpair to Q on Q-T-6 with multiple draws live. Check for pot control.
explanation.rangeContext: Q-T-6 is BB-favored at the top of range with many connected calling combos; BTN's mid pairs prefer checking.
explanation.handLogic: JJ on Q-T-6 is an in-between hand: beats one-pair T-x and underpairs, but loses to QQ, Qx top pair, T9-style two pair, and many gutshot/OESD straight draws. Betting folds out worse and gets called or check-raised by everything that beats us. Check, see a turn (a J-improvement to set is welcome), re-evaluate.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-c-betting JJ because "I have an overpair." JJ here is bluff-catcher, not value.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair on Q-T-6 -> check for pot control.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero has zero clubs on monotone clubs board — no flush equity at all. No straight equity either.

question.prompt: With 4h3h on Jc Tc 8c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with zero clubs on monotone J-high. Check, give up.
explanation.rangeContext: Monotone connected J-high is heavily BB-favored; BTN should check most of the range and abandon air.
explanation.handLogic: 4h3h has no flush equity (no clubs), no straight equity, no pair equity vs almost any continuing range. On monotone J-high BTN should already be checking heavy; pure air gives up.
explanation.commonMistake: Stabbing with low cards on monotone "because BTN should c-bet." Wrong board class — monotone J-high is BB-favored and air gives up.
explanation.takeaway: Air on monotone J-high -> check, give up.

scenarios.blockerNote: Gutshot to 8 makes 7-8-9-T-J straight, but Q-x makes higher straight and 8-x makes the same straight with better equity. Reverse-implied odds.

question.prompt: With 7d6d on Js Tc 9c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Gutshot to 8 on J-T-9 with reverse-implied odds. Check, give up.
explanation.rangeContext: J-T-9 is heavily BB-favored; BTN checks most of the range and gives up dominated draws.
explanation.handLogic: 7d6d gutshot to 8 makes 7-8-9-T-J = a straight, but BB's flatting range contains many Q-x (Q-J, Q-T, Q-9) that already have a higher straight on the same 8 turn — so even when our gutshot hits we're often beat. Plus 0 club equity so flush draw outs are 0. Check, give up cheaply.
explanation.commonMistake: Semi-bluffing the gutshot on J-T-9. The straight is dominated by Q-x; betting bloats the pot for an outdrawn hand.
explanation.takeaway: Dominated gutshot on J-T-9 -> check, give up.

question.prompt: BTN c-bets ~33% pot. What's hero's best action with Th 8h on As 8d 3h?

explanation.short: Middle pair with backdoor flush — call.
