explanation.riverLogic: The Ac is an overcard that hits BTN hard: he now value-bets every Ax he barrelled, plus he can credibly rep the A as a bluff card. The pot-sized bet is value-weighted; pair of jacks beats only the busted bluffs and is now below them in frequency.
explanation.rangeContext: BB called two streets with a J-x pair that was top pair on the flop. The A demotes it to a third-best pair and arms villain with a stack of new value combos.
explanation.handLogic: KcJd is now second pair on an A-high board with no blocker to the value cards. Vs a pot-sized polar bet it is below the bluff-catch threshold.
explanation.commonMistake: Stationing pair of jacks because it was top pair on the flop ignores that the A river flipped the range -- this is the station trap.
explanation.takeaway: A pair demoted by an overcard river, vs a big bet = fold (board changed against you).

scenarios.blockerNote: A in hand makes top pair AND blocks villain AA / strong-Ax value combos, but the weak kicker keeps it borderline vs a pot-sized bet.

question.prompt: Flop Js 8d 5c; turn 3h; river Ac. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Ad Th?

explanation.short: The Ace river paired hero (weak-kicker top pair) -- a call/fold mix that leans call; the A-blocker keeps it from being an auto-fold.
explanation.riverLogic: The Ac gives hero a pair of aces, but with a weak T kicker. Hero holds an A, which blocks villain AA and reduces his strong-Ax value -- pushing his pot-sized range toward busted bluffs that hero beats. Against a value-heavy A-high pot bet, though, weak top pair is right at indifference: solver mixes call and fold.
explanation.rangeContext: BB floated ATs two streets and rivered weak top pair on the scare card others fear. The A-blocker is what keeps this a mix rather than a clear fold; villain still has plenty of better Ax that bet pot.
explanation.handLogic: AdTh is top pair, weak kicker, plus the A-blocker. It loses to AK/AQ/AJ and two-pair/sets, but the blocker tilts villain range toward bluffs enough to make folding too tight.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-folding the rivered ace because the board looks scary over-folds; auto-calling ignores the value-heavy A-high pot bet. The honest answer is a mix.
explanation.takeaway: When the scare card pairs YOU and blocks villain value, weak top pair becomes a call/fold mix -- not an auto-fold.

scenarios.blockerNote: Set of jacks; beats every Ax value-bet, loses only to a set of aces.

question.prompt: Flop Js 8d 5c; turn 3h; river Ac. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Jd Jh?

explanation.short: Set of jacks under an Ace river -- check-raise big for value vs a value-heavy pot-sized bet.
explanation.riverLogic: The Ac armed villain with a wide Ax value range that bets big and calls a raise. Set of jacks beats every Ax, every two-pair, and the lower 55/88 sets; only a set of aces (one A on board, so AA in hand) beats it.
explanation.rangeContext: BB flatted JJ pre and arrives with the effective nuts on a board where villain just turned a stack of Ax into value bets that pay off a raise.
explanation.handLogic: JdJh is a set with near-100% equity vs the betting range; the A river creates the perfect spot to raise because villain is over-valuing his new top pairs.
explanation.sizingLogic: A big check-raise maximizes value from the inelastic Ax value range; a small raise leaves money on the table on a board this wet with villain value.
explanation.commonMistake: Slow-playing the set is acceptable but raising big extracts the most from the Ax range the river just created.
explanation.takeaway: Set under a value-flooding overcard river = check-raise big; villain pays with all his new top pairs.

question.prompt: Flop Js 8d 5c; turn 3h; river Ac. BB folds with Kh Qd vs BTN's river barrel. What is the primary reason?

explanation.short: KQ-high after an Ace river vs a big bet -- fold; capped range with no pair and no useful blocker.
explanation.riverLogic: The Ac favors BTN heavily and the pot-sized bet is value-weighted. KhQd never made a pair, has no flush, and does not block villain key value combos in a way that supports a bluff-raise.
explanation.rangeContext: BB floated two overcards that bricked. On a board where the river armed villain with value and BB stays capped, there is nothing to continue with.
explanation.handLogic: KhQd has zero showdown value (loses to any pair, including the busted bluffs that occasionally have a pair). It is a clean fold.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling KQ-high to "bluff-catch" misreads the hand -- it has no pair, so it loses to literally everything villain value-bets and most of what he bluffs with.
explanation.takeaway: No pair, no blocker, capped range, big bet = range-disadvantage fold (not a bluff-catch).

scenarios.blockerNote: K-high flush; does NOT block the nut flush (no Ah), so villain can still hold the Ah flush.

question.prompt: Flop Qh 9h 4c; turn 2s; river 7h. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Kh Jh?

explanation.short: K-high flush on a flush-complete river vs a big bet -- call; do not raise into the nut flush.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7h puts three hearts out; BB made a K-high flush (Kh Jh + Qh 9h 7h). Vs a pot-sized polar bet this flush beats every non-flush value bet and every bluff, but it is NOT the nuts -- the Ah flush beats it.
explanation.rangeContext: BB held two hearts through two streets and got there. Raising only folds out everything worse and is called by the one hand that beats it (the Ah flush), so the flush plays as a strong bluff-catcher: call.
explanation.handLogic: KhJh is the second-nut flush. Because hero does not hold the Ah, villain still has nut-flush combos; check-raising is thin and loses value to exactly the hands that continue.
explanation.sizingLogic: A small check-raise is a defensible thin-value line vs an over-bluffy opponent, but the GTO line is to call -- raising big into the nut flush is the punt.
explanation.commonMistake: Folding the second-nut flush is a severe over-fold; raising big into the only hand that beats you is a punt.
explanation.takeaway: Non-nut flush on a flush river vs a big bet = call (thin value), not raise.

scenarios.blockerNote: Ah is the nut-flush blocker on hearts; villain cannot hold the nut flush, and hero credibly reps it.

question.prompt: Flop Qh 9h 4c; turn 2s; river 7h. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Ah 8c?

explanation.short: A-high with the lone Ah on a flush river -- check-raise small as a blocker bluff.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7h completes the flush. Hero holds the Ah (one heart, no flush) which blocks villain nut flush combos AND lets hero credibly represent the nut flush. Villain bet-folds his one-pair value and his missed bluffs to a raise.
explanation.rangeContext: BB arrives with a busted hand that has the single most powerful card on the board for a bluff: the nut-flush blocker. This is the river-defining blocker bluff-raise.
explanation.handLogic: Ah8c has no made hand and no showdown value -- calling is impossible (it beats nothing). But the Ah makes it the ideal candidate to turn into a raise-bluff.
explanation.sizingLogic: A small raise is enough to fold out villain bet-folds (Qx/9x value and busted bluffs); a big raise risks more chips than the fold equity is worth.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling A-high here is the punt (zero showdown value); the only profitable non-fold is the blocker raise.
explanation.takeaway: On a flush river, the nut-flush blocker is a bluff-raise (or fold) -- never a call.

scenarios.blockerNote: No heart in hand -- does not block any flush; pair of queens is behind the flush-weighted betting range.

question.prompt: Flop Qh 9h 4c; turn 2s; river 7h. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Qc Js?

explanation.short: Top pair, no heart, on a flush-complete river vs a big bet -- fold; the flush got there and you hold no blocker.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7h completes the front-door flush. Villain pot-sized betting range is now flush-heavy (he barrelled flush draws that just got there) plus his made value; QcJs (pair of queens, no heart) beats only the few pure-air bluffs that bricked even the flush.
explanation.rangeContext: BB held a non-flush top pair through two streets. The river is the worst card for it: the draw villain was barrelling completed, and BB cannot block any of it.
explanation.handLogic: QcJs has top pair but no heart, so it does not block a single flush combo. Vs a big bet on a three-flush board it is a dominated bluff-catcher.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling top pair because "it is top pair" ignores that the flush completed and you block none of it -- this is the classic station trap.
explanation.takeaway: Non-flush pair on a flush-complete river vs a big bet = fold; with no flush blocker you beat almost nothing.

scenarios.blockerNote: Ah is the nut-flush blocker. On this three-heart board a flush needs TWO hearts in hand, so villain has many flush combos; the Ah removes only his nut-flush (Ah-x) combos -- a weak value-blocker, since his King-high and lower flushes remain.

question.prompt: Flop Qh 9h 4c; turn 2s; river 7h. BB calls with Ah 9c vs BTN's river barrel. What is the primary reason?

explanation.short: Pair of nines holding the Ah on a three-heart river -- a blocker-driven call, but only against the medium sizing.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7h puts a third heart out; a flush now needs TWO hearts in hand, so villain has many flush combos. Hero's Ah blocks only the nut-flush (Ah-x) combos -- a weak value-blocker, because the King-high and lower flushes are unblocked. Pair of nines beats only the busted-heart and missed-straight bluffs.
explanation.rangeContext: Among equal bluff-catchers the one holding the key blocker continues -- but here the blocker is weak, so the sizing decides whether the call clears its price.
explanation.handLogic: Ah9c is only middle pair; the Ah is its whole case. Without it the hand folds; with it, against the medium sizing, villain has too few unblocked value combos relative to his bluffs, so hero defends.
explanation.sizingLogic: Against a MEDIUM bet (~66% pot, BB needs ~29%) the pair-of-9 plus the nut-flush blocker clears the threshold by beating the busted bluffs. Against a POT bet the value range is too flush-dense for one weak blocker to matter -- there it would be a fold.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling this versus a pot-sized bet 'because I hold the Ah' -- one weak blocker does not beat a flush-dense value range; the call is sizing-dependent.
explanation.takeaway: A weak nut-flush blocker turns second pair into a call against a medium bet, but not against a pot-sized one.

scenarios.blockerNote: J-T makes the nut straight 7-8-9-T-J; no higher straight exists on this board.

question.prompt: Flop 9d 8c 4h; turn 2s; river 7h. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Jc Td?

explanation.short: Nut straight on a straight-complete river -- check-raise big for value.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7h fills the board 9-8-4-2-7; J-T makes 7-8-9-T-J, the nut straight (no T-J-Q-K-A or higher is possible). Villain still value-bets sets, two-pair and lower straights (T-6, 6-5) that pay a raise.
explanation.rangeContext: BB flatted JT pre, called two barrels with an open-ender, and rivered the nuts. Villain has plenty of second-best value (lower straights, sets) that cannot fold.
explanation.handLogic: JcTd is the stone nuts; there is no protection or reverse-implied concern -- raise as big as villain will pay.
explanation.sizingLogic: A big check-raise maximizes value from sets and lower straights; sizing down only sacrifices value since hero never needs to fold out worse.
explanation.commonMistake: Just calling the nuts leaves enormous value uncollected; folding it would be an absurd punt.
explanation.takeaway: The nut straight on the river = check-raise big; charge the lower straights and sets the maximum.

scenarios.blockerNote: Set of nines; blocks 9-x straights and two-pair slightly, but loses to any made straight.

question.prompt: Flop 9d 8c 4h; turn 2s; river 7h. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with 9h 9s?

explanation.short: Set of nines on a straight-complete river -- call for thin value; do not raise into the made straights.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7h completes 5-6-7-8-9, 6-7-8-9-T and 7-8-9-T-J. Set of nines beats every two-pair, every lower set and all bluffs, but it loses to any T-J, T-6, or 6-5 straight. Vs a medium bet it is a call: it beats the non-straight value and the bluffs, but raising only folds those out and is called by straights.
explanation.rangeContext: BB arrives with a set that was a monster on the turn and is now a medium bluff-catcher because the obvious draw got there.
explanation.handLogic: 9h9s is a set, but on a four-to-a-straight runout it has slipped to thin-value/bluff-catch status -- strong enough to never fold, too weak to raise.
explanation.sizingLogic: A small check-raise is a thin-value option vs a station, but the standard line is call -- a big raise into the straights is a punt that only worse folds to.
explanation.commonMistake: Raising the set here folds out everything it beats and gets called only by the straights that beat it -- over-raising is the critical error.
explanation.takeaway: A set on a four-to-a-straight river is thin value: call, do not raise.

question.prompt: Flop 9d 8c 4h; turn 2s; river 7h. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Ad 8d?

explanation.short: Pair of eights on a straight-complete river vs a medium bet -- fold; the straight got there and the pair is dominated.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7h completes the obvious straights villain was barrelling (T-J, T-6, 6-5). Pair of eights with an A kicker beats only the pure-air bluffs that bricked the straight too; villain value range is straight- and overpair-heavy.
explanation.rangeContext: BB called two streets with middle pair plus an A. The river is the card the draws were chasing -- it favors the betting range, not the bluff-catcher.
explanation.handLogic: Ad8d is second pair; it does not block any straight (no 6, no T, no J) and loses to all of villain made value. The A kicker does not help on the river.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling middle pair because "I have a pair and an ace" ignores that the straight completed and the pair blocks none of villain value -- station trap.
explanation.takeaway: Middle pair on a completed-straight river, no straight blocker = fold.

scenarios.blockerNote: Makes the low straight 5-6-7-8-9; loses to 6-7-8-9-T (T-6) and 7-8-9-T-J (J-T).

question.prompt: Flop 9d 8c 4h; turn 2s; river 7h. BB calls with 6h 5d vs BTN's river barrel. What is the primary reason?

explanation.short: The low end of the straight (5-6-7-8-9) -- call for thin value; raising runs into the higher straights.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7h makes hero a straight, but it is the lowest possible one. It beats sets, two-pair and all bluffs, but loses to T-6 and J-T straights. A raise gets called only by the better straights and folds out everything worse -- so call.
explanation.rangeContext: BB flatted 65-suited, called two streets with a combo draw, and rivered the bottom straight. It is a made hand, but the worst version of it on this board.
explanation.handLogic: 6h5d is a straight, yet against the value-betting range it plays like a thin-value hand: never fold, but never raise (higher straights exist).
explanation.commonMistake: Calling it value_raise_river is the error -- raising the bottom straight only folds worse and is called by better. The reason to call is thin value, not a value raise.
explanation.takeaway: Even a made straight can be only a thin value call when higher straights are possible -- call, do not raise.

scenarios.blockerNote: Top pair, K with J kicker; the paired board mainly kills BB draws, not this made hand.

question.prompt: Flop Kd 7s 3c; turn Qh; river 7d. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Kh Jc?

explanation.short: Top pair vs a small river bet on a paired board -- call to meet minimum defense frequency.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7d pairs the board. Trips (any 7) are rare in villain river-barrelling range, so the pair mainly counterfeits BB busted draws. Vs a small (~33%) bet, MDF is ~75% -- BB must defend almost everything that beats a bluff, and top pair clears that bar easily.
explanation.rangeContext: BB called two streets with KJ. The small sizing screams thin value / give-ups; folding top pair here lets villain auto-profit by betting small with any two.
explanation.handLogic: KhJc is top pair good kicker; it loses only to better Kx, the rare 7x trips, and full houses (K7/Q7/33). Vs a small bet that is far above the call threshold (~20%).
explanation.commonMistake: Folding top pair to a small bet because the board paired is a huge over-fold -- the small sizing is exactly when you must defend widest.
explanation.takeaway: Top pair vs a small bet = call; small sizings demand the highest defense frequency (MDF ~75%).

scenarios.blockerNote: Pair of queens (turned); beats the busted-draw bluffs that a small bet is loaded with.

question.prompt: Flop Kd 7s 3c; turn Qh; river 7d. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Qc Jd?

explanation.short: Second pair vs a small river bet on a paired board -- call; MDF and the busted-draw-heavy range say defend.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7d pairs the board and bricks the draws (the Q turn gave BB plenty of Qx and busted broadway draws). Vs a small bet, villain is betting thin value and a stack of give-ups; pair of queens beats all the give-ups.
explanation.rangeContext: BB turned a pair of queens and faces the smallest sizing. The river barrel range at 33% is bluff-heavy because villain bets small to deny equity cheaply or to value-bet thin; second pair is comfortably above the call threshold.
explanation.handLogic: QcJd is middle pair; it loses to Kx and 7x but beats every busted draw. At ~20% needed equity vs a small bet, it is a clear call.
explanation.commonMistake: Folding second pair to a small bet is the canonical over-fold -- the smaller the bet, the more bluff-catchers must continue.
explanation.takeaway: Second pair vs a small bet on a draw-bricking river = call; over-folding small bets is the most common river leak.

scenarios.blockerNote: Trip sevens; holding a 7 reduces villain trips and lets hero raise for value with little reverse risk.

question.prompt: Flop Kd 7s 3c; turn Qh; river 7d. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with 7h 6h?

explanation.short: Trip sevens on the board-pair river -- check-raise small for value.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7d pairs the board and hands hero trips. Trips beat every Kx and Qx value-bet and all bluffs; only K7/Q7/77 full houses and 33 beat hero, and those are a tiny slice of a river-barrelling range. The small bet means BB is the one who should raise for value here.
explanation.rangeContext: BB called two streets with 76-suited (pair + backdoor) and rivered trips when the board paired. Villain bet small with thin value and give-ups -- the perfect range to raise.
explanation.handLogic: 7h6h is trip sevens; it crushes the value-betting range and only loses to rare full houses. There is no reason to slow-play vs a small bet.
explanation.sizingLogic: A small check-raise gets called by Kx that bet thin and induces hero-calls; a big raise folds out the very value it wants to charge.
explanation.commonMistake: Just calling trips vs a small bet leaves value uncollected; trips want to raise here.
explanation.takeaway: Trips on a board-pair river vs a small bet = check-raise small for value.

question.prompt: Flop Kd 7s 3c; turn Qh; river 7d. BB folds with Js Th vs BTN's river barrel. What is the primary reason?

explanation.short: A busted open-ender (JT) on the board-pair river -- fold; it is a missed draw with no showdown value and no blocker.
explanation.riverLogic: On K-7-3 with a Q turn, JT had an open-ended straight draw (any A or 9 made K-Q-J-T-?). The 7d river pairs the board and completes nothing -- the draw is dead. JT is now J-high air that cannot beat even a bluff.
explanation.rangeContext: BB peeled two streets with the straight draw. The river bricked AND paired; the only question is bluff-raise or fold, and JT holds no useful blocker (it does not block 7x trips or the top Kx value).
explanation.handLogic: JsTh is a busted straight draw -- zero showdown value, zero pair. It is the textbook missed-draw give-up.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling a busted draw because "I have two overcards" is pure spew -- it can never win at showdown. Even on small sizings, air is a fold, not a call.
explanation.takeaway: A busted draw on the river is a give-up (fold) -- never a call; only bluff-raise it with a real blocker.

scenarios.blockerNote: Top pair of aces, Q kicker, holding the Ah. The Ah removes two of villain's three remaining AA combos (Ad is on the board); it does NOT block AK, which uses Ac/As. AQ's edge over AJ is the kicker -- it beats villain's worse-ace value and chops AQ, so it is the stronger continue.

question.prompt: Flop Ad 8s 5c; turn 2h; river Kd. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Ah Qc?

explanation.short: Top-pair ace, Q kicker: the stronger ace bluff-catches the K-scare overbet. AQ beats villain's worse-ace value and the bluffs, so folding over-folds.
explanation.riverLogic: The Kd is the second overcard; villain's overbet (~150% pot, BB needs ~37.5%) is polar -- AK two pair, sets and the rare AA/KK for value, plus busted draws. AQ beats every worse ace, every king-pair and all the bluffs, losing only to two-pair-plus. The Ah removes two of villain's three AA combos (it does not block AK, which never uses the Ah). AQ sits ABOVE the overbet threshold; AhJc, which also loses to villain AQ, is the hand right on it -- so calling is the higher-EV line.
explanation.rangeContext: BB called two streets with AQ. The overbet on the K is exactly where players over-fold; the strong kicker keeps AQ clearly in the continue region rather than a fold.
explanation.handLogic: AhQc is top pair, good kicker. Against the polar overbet it beats the bluffs and every worse ace villain can value-bet; only the kicker separates it from AJ, and that is enough to clear the threshold and call.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-folding top pair to the K overbet is the leak; the stronger ace has to defend at this price.
explanation.takeaway: The stronger ace clears the overbet threshold -- call AQ; the weaker AJ is only a mix.

scenarios.blockerNote: Set of eights; no A/K blocker, so villain keeps full AA/KK combos, but the set still beats every Ax value-bet and all bluffs.

question.prompt: Flop Ad 8s 5c; turn 2h; river Kd. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with 8c 8h?

explanation.short: Middle set facing a K-scare overbet -- call; the overbet is polar, so do not raise into it.
explanation.riverLogic: The Kd is a scare card; vs a polar overbet villain holds either the nuts (AA, KK, or 55/22 sets) or busted bluffs. Set of eights beats every Ax value-bet, every two-pair and all bluffs, but it loses to the over-sets (AA/KK). Raising only folds out the hands it beats and is called by the sets that beat it -- so call.
explanation.rangeContext: BB flatted 88 preflop, called two streets, and arrives with a set on the scare card. Against the polar overbet the set is a strong bluff-catcher, not a raise.
explanation.handLogic: 8c8h is a set; it holds no A/K blocker (villain keeps full AA/KK), but it still crushes the entire Ax-value + bluff portion of the overbet range. Calling captures all of that; raising surrenders it.
explanation.sizingLogic: A small check-raise is a thin-value option vs a bluff-heavy overbetter, but the GTO line is call -- raising into a polar overbet only gets value-owned by the over-sets.
explanation.commonMistake: Folding a set to the overbet is a severe over-fold; raising big into a polar nutted-or-air range is a punt.
explanation.takeaway: A set vs a polar overbet = call (strong bluff-catch); do not raise into nutted-or-air.

scenarios.blockerNote: No nut blocker for this runout (does not block AA/KK/sets); the Qd-Jd backdoor flush and gutshot both bricked.

question.prompt: Flop Ad 8s 5c; turn 2h; river Kd. BTN c-bet small flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets the river. What is BB's best action with Qd Jd?

explanation.short: A busted backdoor (QJ-suited) facing a K overbet -- fold; no showdown value and no nut blocker to raise with.
explanation.riverLogic: QdJd picked up a backdoor diamond draw and a gutshot along the way; the Kd river makes neither (four diamonds is not a flush, and there is no straight). It is Q-high air. Vs a polar overbet it can only bluff-raise or fold.
explanation.rangeContext: BB floated with backdoor equity that all bricked. To bluff-raise an overbet hero needs to block villain nutted combos (AA/KK/sets) -- QdJd blocks none of them, so the raise has no credible story.
explanation.handLogic: QdJd has no pair, no flush (only four diamonds), no straight, and no relevant blocker. It is a pure give-up.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling Q-high to "see a showdown" is impossible -- it beats nothing; bluff-raising without a nut blocker into an overbet is stack suicide.
explanation.takeaway: Busted air with no nut blocker, vs an overbet = fold; no showdown value means no call, and no blocker means no raise.

scenarios.blockerNote: Top pair of aces, J kicker, with the Ah. The Ah removes most of villain's AA combos (Ad on board); it does not block AK. AJ also loses to villain's AQ, so it is the weaker ace -- right at the overbet threshold.

question.prompt: Flop Ad 8s 5c; turn 2h; river Kd. BB calls with Ah Jc vs BTN's river barrel. What is the primary reason?

explanation.short: Top pair, weak kicker (AJ) with the Ah vs a K-scare overbet -- the bottom of the defending ace class, a genuine call/fold mix.
explanation.riverLogic: The Kd overbet (~150% pot, BB needs ~37.5%) is polar: AK/sets/AA-KK for value, busted draws as bluffs. AhJc beats the bluffs and the worse aces, but unlike AhQc it also loses to villain's AQ value. The Ah reduces villain AA (it does not block AK). That extra losing combo puts AJ right at the ~37.5% indifference line -- a true call/fold mix, not a clean call.
explanation.rangeContext: AJ is the bottom of BB's defending ace class here. Folding it entirely over-folds; calling it always over-defends; the solver mixes. The over-fold trap is the secondary lesson -- the scariest sizing is where folding too much costs the most.
explanation.handLogic: AhJc is top pair, weak kicker. It is a clear notch below AQ -- it loses to villain AQ where AQ chops or wins -- so it sits at indifference: mix call and fold.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating AJ as a pure call or a pure fold -- at the overbet threshold it is genuinely mixed.
explanation.takeaway: The weakest defending ace vs an overbet is a mix, not a clean call; only the stronger kicker (AQ) clears the threshold outright.

scenarios.blockerNote: No relevant blockers; with a tiny price the underpair only needs to beat the busted-barrel bluffs.

question.prompt: Flop Ac 7d 4s; turn 9h; river 2c. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets about a third of pot (small) on the river. What is BB best action with 8d 8c?

explanation.short: Underpair vs a tiny river bet -- the price alone makes it a call.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2c is a double blank: no flush (only two clubs), no straight, no new pair. Ranges are unchanged from the turn. At about a third of pot BTN can bet a wide, bluff-heavy range, so the small price (about 4-to-1) is the whole story.
explanation.rangeContext: BB arrives capped after calling two streets, but a third-pot bet only asks BB to defend the very bottom of its range; an underpair clears that bar easily.
explanation.handLogic: 88 beats every busted barrel (KQ/KJ/QJ/JT that whiffed) and the occasional thin 7x. It loses to Ax, 9x and sets, but at 4-to-1 it needs to be good only about one time in five.
explanation.commonMistake: Folding small pairs to a tiny river bet -- over-folding to cheap sizings hands BTN an automatic profit.
explanation.takeaway: Small sizing, small required equity: price an underpair in as a pure pot-odds call.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds an ace, so it blocks a little BTN value, but the call is driven by MDF, not by card removal.

question.prompt: Flop Ac 7d 4s; turn 9h; river 2c. BB calls Ad 6c vs BTN two-thirds-pot river bet. What is the primary reason?

explanation.short: Weak top pair calls a two-thirds barrel to meet minimum defense frequency.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2c is a blank on Ac 7d 4s 9h: no flush, no straight. Versus two-thirds pot BB must continue with roughly 60% of its range, and its many aces are the backbone of that defense.
explanation.rangeContext: BB holds a lot of aces by the river. Folding the weak ones would drop BB far below MDF and let BTN barrel any two cards profitably, so the bottom of the Ax class still defends a two-thirds bet.
explanation.handLogic: A6 beats worse aces, every pair below it and all busted bluffs; it loses to better aces and sets. The smaller size pulls in more bluffs, so defending top pair keeps BB at MDF rather than over-folding a hand this strong.
explanation.commonMistake: Folding all your weak aces to a manageable barrel -- that defends well below MDF and prints money for a triple-barreller.
explanation.takeaway: MDF over hand-love: a weak ace is still top pair, and you cannot fold the top-pair class to a two-thirds bet without over-folding.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds the As (blocks some AK/AQ value) and the Qc; a strong top pair that must not be folded to a polar bet.

question.prompt: Flop Ac 7d 4s; turn 9h; river 2c. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets pot (large) on the river. What is BB best action with As Qc?

explanation.short: Top pair, good kicker is a mandatory call versus a pot-sized third barrel -- folding over-folds.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2c bricks Ac 7d 4s 9h: no flush, no straight. A pot-sized bet is polar -- strong aces and sets for value, busted broadways and gutshots as bluffs -- and a balanced BTN must bluff enough that a strong ace has to call.
explanation.rangeContext: BB is capped but holds plenty of aces. AQ sits near the top of that bluff-catching class; if BB folds hands this strong it defends far too little and BTN auto-profits.
explanation.handLogic: AQ beats AJ, AT, every worse ace BTN might bet thin, and the entire busted-bluff bucket. It loses only to AK and sets, so versus a pot bet that must contain bluffs to stay balanced it is a clear call.
explanation.commonMistake: Folding good top pair to a big river bet "because it looks scary" -- the classic over-fold leak that makes barrelling free.
explanation.takeaway: Big bet, strong bluff-catcher: call. Over-folding top pair is exactly what a polar barreller wants.

scenarios.blockerNote: KQ blocks a few BTN value combos (AK/KK/QQ) but holds no pair and beats nothing that bets; it is a pure give-up.

question.prompt: Flop Ac 7d 4s; turn 9h; river 2c. BB folds Kc Qd vs BTN pot-sized river bet. What is the primary reason?

explanation.short: King-high air folds to a big barrel -- a capped range cannot manufacture a call here.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2c bricks the dry board. KQ never made a pair and never had a draw; it is simply the bottom of a capped calling range that arrives with no showdown value.
explanation.rangeContext: BB called flop and turn with pairs and draws; by the river the missed broadway combos are the worst hands BB holds, and BTN attacks exactly them.
explanation.handLogic: KQ beats nothing BTN value-bets and only ties or loses to other air. There is nothing to bluff-catch with -- holding the K and Q is a tiny blocker note, not a reason to call.
explanation.commonMistake: Talking yourself into a "blocker" call with two overcards -- blocking a few value combos does not give a no-pair hand a price to call a pot bet.
explanation.takeaway: When your range is capped and your hand is air, fold; blockers do not turn no-pair into a call.

scenarios.blockerNote: Ah is the nut-flush blocker: BTN is far less likely to hold the nut flush (Ah-x), so a check-raise credibly represents it and folds out one-pair value and weak made hands.

question.prompt: Flop Kh 9h 5c; turn 4d; river 2h. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets pot (large) on the river. What is BB best action with Ah Jc?

explanation.short: A-high with the nut-flush blocker check-raises the river as a bluff -- or folds; never calls.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2h puts a third heart out, completing the flush draw. The board now favors whoever can credibly hold a flush; holding the Ah means BTN almost never has the nut flush, so a raise tells a believable story.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN bets a large polar range: made flushes and strong hands for value, plus busted hearts as bluffs. BB has no showdown value here, so the only ways to win are to raise BTN off the non-flush portion or to give up.
explanation.handLogic: AhJc has no pair and cannot win at showdown, so calling is pointless. But the Ah removes BTN nut-flush combos, so a check-raise pressures his one-pair value and missed bluffs into folding.
explanation.sizingLogic: A small check-raise is enough to fold out non-flush value and weak made hands while risking the minimum; a big raise over-invests, since the only hands that continue are the flushes that already beat us.
explanation.commonMistake: Flatting A-high "to see a showdown" -- with zero showdown value the choice is raise-as-a-bluff or fold, and calling just burns a bet.
explanation.takeaway: Busted hand plus the right blocker: the river is for bluff-raising or folding, never calling.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds the Ah with a second heart: this IS the nut flush (Ace-high), unbeatable on an unpaired board -- raise for maximum value.

question.prompt: Flop Kh 9h 5c; turn 4d; river 2h. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets pot (large) on the river. What is BB best action with Ah 3h?

explanation.short: The nut flush on an unpaired board -- check-raise big for maximum value.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2h completes a three-flush and gives Ah-x the nut flush. The board is unpaired, so no full house is possible: the nut flush is the effective nuts and nothing beats it.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN large bet contains lower flushes, two pair and sets that cannot fold easily on this runout; against that calling range a raise is pure value.
explanation.handLogic: Ah3h is the best possible hand. Flat-calling wins only the one street BTN already bet; check-raising charges the lower flushes and strong made hands that will pay.
explanation.sizingLogic: Go big: BTN lower flushes and strong hands are inelastic here, so a large check-raise maximizes value; a small raise leaves money on the table against a range that would have called more.
explanation.commonMistake: Slow-playing the nuts on a wet board -- flat-calling forfeits a full street of value against a range full of second-best flushes.
explanation.takeaway: With the effective nuts on an unpaired flush board, raise big; the only real mistake is failing to get the money in.

scenarios.blockerNote: No flush blocker (no heart) and a weak kicker; nothing tips the spot off its knife-edge, so the solver mixes.

question.prompt: Flop Kh 9h 5c; turn 4d; river 2h. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BTN now bets pot (large) on the river. What is BB best action with Kc Td?

explanation.short: Top pair, weak kicker, no blocker on a flush board versus a big bet -- a genuine call/fold mix.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2h completes the flush, so BTN large bet is polar: flushes and better for value, busted hearts as bluffs. A bare top pair beats the bluffs and loses to the value, with no blocker to break the tie.
explanation.rangeContext: BB must defend some top pairs versus the large bet, but not all of them; King-Ten with no heart is right on the indifference line, so it splits between call and fold.
explanation.handLogic: King-Ten beats every busted heart and worse one-pair, and loses to all flushes plus better kings and two pair. Without a heart to block the nut flush nothing nudges it clearly one way, so both calling and folding are correct at some frequency.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating a knife-edge hand as a pure call or pure fold -- and especially raising it, which only folds out worse and gets called by better.
explanation.takeaway: When a bluff-catcher is genuinely indifferent and holds no relevant blocker, mixing call and fold is the answer; raising is the one clear error.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds two middle hearts so it blocks some lower flushes, but not the Ah or Qh that beat it; this is a call-for-value hand, not a raise.

question.prompt: Flop Kh 9h 5c; turn 4d; river 2h. BB calls (does not raise) Jh Th vs BTN two-thirds-pot river bet. What is the primary reason?

explanation.short: A middle flush calls (does not raise) a two-thirds bet -- thin value, since only better flushes continue to a raise.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2h completes a three-flush. JhTh makes a middling flush: ahead of one-pair value and the busted hearts that bet, but behind the Ah and Qh flushes.
explanation.rangeContext: Versus a medium bet BB is not capped at the flush level; a made flush is near the top of BB range and wants to get value -- but only from worse.
explanation.handLogic: JhTh beats every non-flush hand BTN bets plus the few lower flushes, and loses only to the Ah or Qh flushes. Calling collects from all of that; raising would fold out the one-pair value and get called only by the flushes that beat it.
explanation.commonMistake: Raising a non-nut flush "because it is a flush" -- that turns a value hand into a bluff-catcher by folding out everything it beats.
explanation.takeaway: Hold a made-but-beatable hand? Call to keep worse hands betting; do not raise into the few hands that have you crushed.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds the Th: the only straights on this board (QT, T7) both need a ten, so hero trims each by about a quarter. But the busted open-enders KT and AT use the same tens, so the blocker cuts bluffs too -- a two-sided nudge, unlike the Ah on a flush board.

question.prompt: Flop Jc 9d 8s; turn 4h; river 2c. BB calls Th 9h vs BTN's two-thirds-pot river bet. What is the primary reason?

explanation.short: Second pair with the T straight-blocker vs a medium third barrel -- a genuine call/fold mix; the blocker is a nudge, not the reason.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2c is a brick: no flush ever existed on this rainbow-flop runout, and the only straights (QT and T7, both live since the J-9-8 flop) need a ten. Hero's Th removes one of villain's four tens -- about a quarter of the QT and T7 value -- but the busted open-enders KT and AT use the same tens, so the blocker removes bluffs as well. Value and bluffs shrink together and the ratio barely moves: pair of nines beats every busted draw, loses to every value hand, and sits right at the ~29% price a two-thirds bet sets. That is indifference, not a blocker-driven call.
explanation.rangeContext: Vs a medium bet BB defends roughly 60% of its range, filled first by straights, two pair, Jx and TT. Pair of nines is below that median, exactly where MDF runs out -- the textbook mixing region.
explanation.handLogic: T9s arrived as second pair plus an open-ended draw (J-T-9-8 needed a Q or a 7), called flop and turn naturally, and missed. At showdown it is a pure bluff-catcher: it beats KQ, AQ, KT, AT, 76 and every other busted draw, and loses to all Jx, TT, two pair, sets and straights. Compare Q9s: same pair of nines, and its Q still trims the QT nut straight -- both hands end up mixing, which shows that no single straight-card removes enough combos to flip a verdict.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating the Th like the Ah on a three-flush board and calling purely because of the blocker. Picking blocker_bluff_catch_river here overrates a two-sided 25% effect: on a flush board the Ah deletes 100% of the nut flush and blocks no bluffs; a straight-blocker cuts a quarter of the straights AND a share of the busted open-enders.
explanation.takeaway: A straight-blocker is one of four cards and sits on both sides of villain's range -- a two-sided ~25% nudge that turns close hands into mixes, never the one-sided 100% lock of the nut-flush Ah.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holding a K and an 8 slightly thins the Kx and 8x calling regions, but the value region left (KQ, KJ, KT, K9) is wide enough that the bet stands on its own.

question.prompt: Flop Kh 8d 3s; turn 6c; river 2d. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Kc 8c?

explanation.short: Top two on a brick runout: bet big and charge every Kx.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2d is a pure brick: no flush arrives (two diamonds only) and no straight completes -- 54 needed to get there via 2-3-4-5-6 but 54 never had a one-card draw on the K-8-3 flop, so the line filtered it out. Ranges are frozen from the turn and hero still holds the best two-pair.
explanation.rangeContext: BB check-called twice on a dry board, which caps the range around Kx one-pair, 8x, and the odd stubborn pocket pair. Slow-played sets of 3s or 6s exist but are rare and part check-raise earlier.
explanation.handLogic: K8 beats every one-pair hand that took this line -- KQ, KJ, KT, K9 and all 8x -- and beats the turned two-pair K6 as well (8 outkicks 6 as the second pair). Only 33, 66 and the rare 22 that peeled twice beat it.
explanation.sizingLogic: Big sizing (~75%) is the point: Kx has bluff-catch equity against the missed-draw story and pays a large bet after calling two streets. Small sizing leaves a full street of value unclaimed against a range that rarely folds a K.
explanation.commonMistake: Betting small "to keep everything in" -- the hands that call small (Kx) also call big here, so the discount is pure lost value.
explanation.takeaway: When the runout bricks and your two-pair beats the whole calling region, size up: the third barrel is where the money is made.

scenarios.blockerNote: The two 9s remove most 9x two-pair combos from BB, concentrating the calling range on Ax -- exactly the region a big bet targets.

question.prompt: Flop As 9h 5d; turn Qc; river 2h. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with 9s 9c?

explanation.short: Set of 9s under the ace: big value bet into the Ax-heavy check-call range.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2h changes almost nothing: the only new arrival is the rare 43-suited wheel (A-2-3-4-5) that peeled a bare gutter on the flop and then faced a turn barrel -- a heavily discounted holding. Hero's set is effectively the top of the ranges that reach this river.
explanation.rangeContext: BB's check-call check-call line on an A-high board is saturated with Ax: AJ, AT, weak suited aces, plus some Qx that picked up the turn pair. All of it bluff-catches; none of it beats a set.
explanation.handLogic: Middle set beats every Ax one-pair, every Qx, and both two-pair combos (AQ, A9 is blocked). Only 55 played this passively ever beats hero, and most sets check-raise before the river.
explanation.sizingLogic: Big (~75%) extracts from Ax which feels pot-committed after two calls. An overbet is a defensible polar alternative -- villain's Ax is a strong catcher -- but big keeps the weaker aces in. Small is a clear underperformance against a range this sticky.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking back the set because "the wheel got there" -- a one-combo-class scare against dozens of Ax combos is exactly the over-caution the river rewards you for ignoring.
explanation.takeaway: Count the region that pays you (all Ax) against the region that beats you (rare slow-played sets): when the ratio is this lopsided, bet big.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holding an A and a K removes the strongest bluff-catch combos (other AK) and leaves the calling range weighted to AQ/AJ/AT -- all of which hero beats.

question.prompt: Flop Ah Kc 7d; turn 7h; river 3s. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. BTN holds Ad Ks and the best line is a big bet. WHY?

explanation.short: Top two on A-K-7-7-3: the Ax region check-calls twice and pays a third big bet.
explanation.riverLogic: The 3s is a blank on a board that paired its third card. AK's two pair (aces and kings) still beats every one-pair Ax that took the check-call check-call line: AQ, AJ, AT and the suited wheel aces that peeled.
explanation.rangeContext: Trips are the only real threat and the combos are thin: 7x that check-calls an A-K-high flop is basically 76s/75s. Most 7x folded the flop, and A7s/K7s two-pairs part check-raise the turn when the 7 pairs.
explanation.handLogic: Beats all Ax one-pair; loses only to the few surviving 7x-trips and slow-played boats. The dominated calling region is several times wider than the beaten one.
explanation.sizingLogic: Big sizing charges AQ/AJ maximum; they are near the top of villain's range and do not fold to one more barrel. Overbetting is unnecessary risk against the trips tail; small sizing gives the dominated aces a discount they would never demand.
explanation.commonMistake: Freezing at the paired 7 and checking back -- surrendering a full street from the widest value region in the tree because of two or three suited combos.
explanation.takeaway: A paired mid-card rarely turns a thick-value river into a check: count the trips combos before you downgrade the hand.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero holds a K and a J, halving villain's KJ (chop) combos; QJ and JT two-pair-or-pair regions are also thinned. That trims some calls, but the nut hand still demands maximum pressure.

question.prompt: Flop Qc 9d 5s; turn Th; river 3c. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Kh Jd?

explanation.short: Turned nut straight, brick river: overbet the capped check-call range.
explanation.riverLogic: The 3c is a total brick. Hero's K-high straight (K-Q-J-T-9) made on the turn is still the pure nuts: no flush is possible and no higher straight exists because the board never brings the A-K connection needed.
explanation.rangeContext: BB's two check-calls cap the range at one pair, two pair (QT, Q9, T9) and stubborn draws that missed. A range this capped against the literal nuts is the textbook polar overbet target: every made hand bluff-catches, nothing beats you.
explanation.handLogic: The nuts win against 100% of villain's continues. The only "cost" is the chop against KJ, which hero's own K and J make rare.
explanation.sizingLogic: A pot-sized-plus bet is polar and villain knows it -- but two-pair and strong Qx still pay it off at a meaningful frequency after committing two streets. Any smaller size sells the best possible hand at a discount.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking back the nuts "to be tricky" -- there is no later street; the river is the last chance to be paid and the check burns the entire bet for zero information gain.
explanation.takeaway: Nut hand + capped range + brick river = overbet. The polar sizing is the whole point of having barrelled.

scenarios.blockerNote: No relevant removal: hero's J touches nothing in villain's continue range. The bet is justified by kicker domination, not blockers.

question.prompt: Flop Ac 8h 5d; turn 2c; river 7s. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Ah Jc?

explanation.short: Top pair J-kicker: small value bet targets the wide dominated Ax region.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7s completes only the deep-gutter straights (46, 96) that check-called the flop with four-out draws and then faced a turn barrel -- a thin, heavily filtered slice. The card leaves the Ax ladder untouched, and that ladder is where the money is.
explanation.rangeContext: BB's check-call check-call range on A-8-5-2 is dense with dominated aces: AT, A9, A6s, A4s, A3s all reach the river and all beat a bluff, so they call one modest bet. The two-pair promotions (A8, A7, A5s, A2s) are real but partly check-raise earlier streets.
explanation.handLogic: AJ beats AT, A9, A6s, A4s, A3s and every 8x. It loses to AQ/AK (which mostly bet-raise earlier or were 3-bet preflop), the promoted two-pairs, and the rare rivered 46s/96s. The dominated region is clearly wider.
explanation.sizingLogic: A third-pot bet is the merge price: dominated Ax calls it almost universally, while a big bet folds exactly the hands hero beats and keeps exactly the ones that beat him. Thin value lives or dies on sizing discipline.
explanation.commonMistake: Blasting 75% "because top pair" -- against this calling range the big bet turns a value hand into a self-isolation play.
explanation.takeaway: Thin value wants a wide net and a low price: bet small enough that every dominated ace pays.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero's Q blocks QJ/QT floats that already folded; no meaningful removal of the calling range. The case rests on the kicker ladder.

question.prompt: Flop Ad Td 6c; turn 3h; river 8s. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Ac Qh?

explanation.short: AQ on a runout that promoted a few two-pairs: still a small-bet value spot.
explanation.riverLogic: The 8s brings 97 in for the 6-7-8-9-T straight, but 97 held a bare gutter on the flop and faced a turn barrel with nothing -- the line has filtered most of it out. The 8 also promotes A8s to two pair. Both arrivals are thin; the dominated-ace ladder below AQ is not.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain's river check range keeps AJ, A9s, A7s and similar dominated aces plus Tx that refuses to fold. AT flopped two pair but check-raises the flop or turn often enough to be discounted here.
explanation.handLogic: AQ beats AJ, A9s, A7s, A5s-type aces and all Tx. It loses to AK (partly 3-bet preflop), AT, the rivered A8s and the rare 97s. Combo for combo the dominated callers still outnumber the promoted hands.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small keeps AJ/A9s/Tx in the calling pool. Sizing up folds the dominated aces and isolates against exactly the promoted two-pair region -- the classic thin-value sizing error.
explanation.commonMistake: Letting the 8 scare you into checking: the promoted combos are few and the ladder below you still pays a third of pot.
explanation.takeaway: Rank the arrivals against the ladder you dominate; when the ladder is wider, keep betting -- just keep it small.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero's Q reduces the QJ two-pair combos that beat him; the K blocks KQ (chop) and some KJ floats. Net removal slightly favors betting.

question.prompt: Flop Qd 7s 4c; turn Jh; river 2s. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. BTN holds Kc Qc and the best line is a small bet. WHY?

explanation.short: KQ top pair: the Qx/Jx ladder calls one small bet; only QJ and slow-plays beat it.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2s is a brick. The turn J is the card that mattered: it promoted QJ to two pair and gave JT/J9s a pair to check-call with. Hero's KQ still sits above the entire one-pair field.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain check-called twice, holding Qx (QT, Q9s), now Jx (JT, J9s), and busted gutters like T9/AT that fold to any bet. Sets of 7s or 4s that never raised are rare.
explanation.handLogic: KQ dominates QT, Q9s and beats every Jx. It loses to QJ, AQ (partly 3-bet preflop) and slow-played sets -- a short list against a wide dominated region.
explanation.sizingLogic: The dominated Qx/Jx hands call a third of pot and fold to most larger sizes; polar sizing here would fold out the entire region hero profits from while isolating against QJ exactly.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking back because "QJ got there" -- surrendering value to a two-combo-class fear while QT/Q9s/JT are ready to pay a small bet.
explanation.takeaway: Thin value is a counting exercise: dominated callers wide, beating hands narrow, price small.

scenarios.blockerNote: The J kicker beats the AT/A8s slice that calls a small bet; hero blocks none of the promoted two-pair combos (A9, A7, A4, A2), which is what keeps the spot at indifference rather than a pure bet.

question.prompt: Flop Ac 7d 4s; turn 9h; river 2c. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Ah Jh?

explanation.short: AJ at the exact thin-value boundary: bet small and check back are both fine.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2c is a brick on the same runout where BB students learn to defend 88 against a small bet -- this is the bettor's seat of that spot. The question is whether AJ still clears the thin-value bar, and the honest answer is: only just.
explanation.rangeContext: The dominated callers are AT, A8s, A6s, A5s and stubborn 9x. The promoted two-pairs are A9 (the big one -- A9o defends preflop and the turn 9 made it two pair), A7, A4s, A2s. Part of the A9 region check-raises the turn, which is what keeps the bet alive at all.
explanation.handLogic: AJ beats AT/A8s/A6s/A5s/9x/88-type hands and loses to A9/A7/A4s/A2s plus AQ/AK remnants. The two regions are close enough in weight that neither pure line is an error.
explanation.sizingLogic: If betting, it must be small: the dominated slice calls a third of pot and nothing wider. Big sizing flips the spot into pure self-isolation and is the one real mistake available.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating a genuine mix as a forced bet and then reaching for a big size "since we are betting anyway".
explanation.takeaway: Some rivers are decided by one kicker step: AJ here is a coin-flip between small bet and check -- both are professional; big is not.

scenarios.blockerNote: The Ah is the key card: it removes every Ah-x nut flush from villain's range, so the strongest hands that could pay or trap simply do not exist. Hero's own missed nut-flush draw is also the most credible flush he can represent.

question.prompt: Flop Kh Th 4c; turn 7h; river 2s. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Ah 5s?

explanation.short: Busted nut-flush draw with the Ah: the premier polar bluff on a three-heart board.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2s changes nothing; the hand was decided on the 7h turn that completed the flush hero was drawing to. Having barrelled the turn with the nut draw, hero arrives with ace-high and the single best card to hold when bluffing: the Ah itself.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain's check-call check-call range is pairs (Kx, Tx) plus modest flushes that chose not to raise, plus a few one-heart floats that missed. Against the pair-heavy core, ace-high wins almost nothing at showdown -- the check-back realizes close to zero.
explanation.handLogic: A5 has no pair and beats only the rare busted Q-high float. The choice is between giving up a hand with near-zero showdown value and betting it as the top of the bluff ladder. The Ah makes it the top: villain can never hold the nut flush.
explanation.sizingLogic: The overbet tells the one story consistent with the whole line: hero barrelled a flush draw and got there. Flushes and sets bet huge here; pairs face an impossible price. Smaller sizes let Kx call profitably and waste the blocker.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking back "because ace-high sometimes wins" -- against a range that check-called two streets, it almost never does; the hand's value is its blocker, not its showdown weight.
explanation.takeaway: Bluff the combos whose cards delete villain's best calls: the Ah on a three-heart board is the classic.

scenarios.blockerNote: The J halves villain's JT combos -- the turned nut straight that check-calls to trap -- and the K removes KQ, the sturdiest top-pair bluff-catcher. Both of villain's best continues are cut before the bet is even made.

question.prompt: Flop Qs 9d 4h; turn 8c; river 2s. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Kc Jc?

explanation.short: Busted double-gutter with K and J blockers: bet big, both key blockers working.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2s bricks a runout where the 8 completed JT's straight (8-9-T-J-Q). Hero's KJ missed its own gutter to the same T and holds king-high -- no pair, no showdown value against a range that called two streets.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain's river checks are Qx pairs, 9x/8x pairs and the occasional slow-played JT straight. The straights and the best Qx (KQ) are exactly the combos hero's own cards deplete.
explanation.handLogic: King-high beats nothing that check-called twice. With zero showdown value the only question is bluff quality, and KJ ranks near the top: it blocks the trap (JT) and the stubbornest call (KQ) simultaneously.
explanation.sizingLogic: Big polar sizing pressures the one-pair core (Qx, 9x) that must decide whether hero has the straight he is representing. Small sizing offers those pairs a price they will always take.
explanation.commonMistake: Giving up because the 8 "got there for villain" -- the same card is hero's story, and hero's blockers make villain's version of it rare.
explanation.takeaway: The best bluffs block the calls AND the traps: when both of your cards do work, apply maximum pressure.

scenarios.blockerNote: The Qh removes every Qh-x second-nut flush from villain's range; combined with the busted open-ender there is no showdown value to protect.

question.prompt: Flop Kh Th 4c; turn 7h; river 2s. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. BTN holds Qh Js and the best line is a big bet. WHY?

explanation.short: Busted combo-draw holding the Qh: the second-nut-flush blocker makes the bluff.
explanation.riverLogic: Hero barrelled the turn with the second-nut flush draw plus an open-ender and missed everything. Queen-high never wins against a range that check-called two streets on this board; the hand's entire remaining equity is fold equity.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain holds Kx/Tx pairs, medium flushes that flatted the turn, and one-heart floats that missed. The Qh strips the QhXh flushes out of that middle band, tilting villain toward hands that struggle against a big polar bet.
explanation.handLogic: No pair, no showdown value; the combo is either a bluff or dead money. Its blocker quality (second-nut flush card) puts it clearly on the bluff side of the give-up line.
explanation.sizingLogic: Flushes bet big on this river, so the bluff bets big; a small "cheap" bluff tells a story no value hand tells and gets called by every pair. Thin value is obviously not an available interpretation with queen-high.
explanation.commonMistake: Checking with the idea that queen-high "might win": the check-call check-call range is pair-dense; it does not.
explanation.takeaway: On flush-completed rivers, rank your bluffs by the flush cards you hold: nut blocker first, second-nut blocker close behind.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero holds no heart: every busted heart draw villain check-folds with is still in the range, keeping the fold region at full width. Holding a heart here would block the very hands that fold -- the counterintuitive reason 76s with no heart outranks a random busted heart as a bluff.

question.prompt: Flop 9h 8h 3d; turn Kc; river 2c. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with 7s 6s?

explanation.short: Busted open-ender with no heart: unblocking the folds is the hidden edge.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2c misses every draw: hearts never came and the 9-8 straights stayed incomplete. Hero's 76s open-ender is now seven-high -- literally unable to win at showdown against any hand that called two streets.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain's river check range splits into made pairs (9x, 8x, some Kx that got there on the turn) and busted heart draws that check-call flop, peel some turns, and check-fold the river. The bluff profits exactly when that second group is as wide as possible.
explanation.handLogic: Seven-high has zero showdown value, so checking realizes nothing. Betting wins whenever villain folds, and hero's specific combo -- no heart, no 9, no 8 -- leaves villain's folding hands untouched while barely touching the calls.
explanation.sizingLogic: Big sizing pressures the one-pair core into a genuine decision against the flush-and-straight story hero has told since the flop. Small invites every pair to call and turns the bluff into a donation.
explanation.commonMistake: Picking bluffs by "I have a draw blocker" alone: holding a heart here would REMOVE villain's folding hands -- the exact opposite of what a bluff wants.
explanation.takeaway: A great bluff unblocks the folds. No-pair, no-blocker-of-folds combos like this are better candidates than they look.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero's 7 and 6 sit inside villain's own busted-draw region (76, 65 folded or never arrived), thinning the folds, and the 7c also clips a share of the 97 turned straights. Trimming a value combo or two does not uncap the range -- Q9 straights, J8/T8 two pairs and the remaining 97 all still check to trap -- while the foldable region stays thin AND partly blocked. Net profile: still a bad bluff.

question.prompt: Flop Jd Th 3c; turn 8s; river 4d. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with 7c 6c?

explanation.short: Missed gutter on a straight-heavy runout: this is a give-up, not a hero bluff.
explanation.riverLogic: The 4d is a brick, but the damage is structural: the turn 8 completed 9-7 and Q-9 straights that live comfortably inside BB's check-call range, and BB's two-pair region (J8, T8) grew on the same card. The river check from villain is not weakness here -- it is often a trap posture on a board that smashed the check-call line.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain's range is uncapped: turned straights and two-pairs check to induce, while the folding region -- busted low gutters -- is thin and partly blocked by hero's own cards.
explanation.handLogic: Seven-high never wins at showdown, but that alone does not make a bluff right: betting needs folds, and this range does not fold enough. The combo also fails the blocker test in both directions.
explanation.sizingLogic: There is no good size for a bad bluff. The big barrel is the expensive version of the mistake, which is why it grades worst; the small stab is the cheaper version of the same leak.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-bluffing every missed draw "because we have no showdown value" -- give-up discipline is folding the bluff itself when the range and blockers say no.
explanation.takeaway: Bluff selection has a reject pile. Uncapped villain range plus fold-region blockers means check and move on.

scenarios.blockerNote: The K and T block KJ, JT and T9-type busted broadways -- on this dry runout those are most of what villain can fold. Blocking the fold region is the disqualifier.

question.prompt: Flop Ah Qd 7s; turn 7d; river 3c. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Ks Ts?

explanation.short: Busted royal gutter into an Ax-heavy range: the folds are blocked, give up.
explanation.riverLogic: The 3c is a blank on A-Q-7-7. Hero barrelled the gutter to the jack and missed; king-high loses to every pair in a range built almost entirely of Ax, Qx and the surviving 7x trips.
explanation.rangeContext: BB's check-call check-call range on an A-Q-high board is top-heavy with aces that do not fold to a third barrel at any reasonable price -- they beat the missed-draw story too easily. The hands that CAN fold are busted KJ/JT/T9 broadway draws, and hero holds two of the four key cards himself.
explanation.handLogic: No pair, no showdown value, no fold equity worth buying: the three reasons to put money in are all absent at once.
explanation.sizingLogic: A big bet into an uncapped, ace-rich range is the costliest available action -- that is the critical grade. The small stab fares little better; it just loses less.
explanation.commonMistake: Talking yourself into "one more barrel" because you hold overcards to the middle card: overcards to a paired, ace-high board are not equity.
explanation.takeaway: When your own cards shrink villain's folding range, your bluff is pre-refuted. Check and keep the 20 BB.

scenarios.blockerNote: The Q and J block QT/JT busted gutters -- villain's main folding hands on this runout -- while the completed 87 straight and the new 6x trips are untouched. Wrong-direction blockers again.

question.prompt: Flop Kd 9h 5s; turn 6h; river 6c. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. BTN holds Qh Jh and the best line is to check back. WHY?

explanation.short: Busted flush draw plus gutter on a paired river: every reason to bluff fails.
explanation.riverLogic: The 6c pairs the turn card. The 6h turn had completed 8-7's straight (5-6-7-8-9) and handed 76s/86s a pair that just became trips; the river makes villain's check range MORE trappy, not less. Hero's combo missed the hearts and the ten.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain check-called twice and now holds Kx, 9x, turned straights and fresh trips. The folding region is busted QT/JT gutters and one-heart floats -- thin, and hero's own Q and J block a chunk of it.
explanation.handLogic: Queen-high wins no showdown that matters. The right-action-wrong-reason trap is "check because queen-high sometimes wins" -- it does not against this range; the check is right because the BLUFF is wrong: uncapped range, blocked folds, no credible story.
explanation.sizingLogic: The tempting line is a big scare-card barrel at the paired 6, but the 6 is villain's card, not hero's: it improves the check-call range and the story collapses.
explanation.commonMistake: Bluffing at every paired river "because it looks scary" -- pairing the TURN card strengthens exactly the hands that check-called the turn.
explanation.takeaway: Before bluffing, ask whose range the river card helped. If the answer is villain, give up.

scenarios.blockerNote: The K and Q block QJ and KJ -- the busted gutters villain folds -- while the 8-6 straight the river completed and the Ax region are unblocked. The overbet temptation runs into the worst possible removal profile.

question.prompt: Flop Th 9d 3c; turn As; river 7d. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Kd Qd?

explanation.short: The polar overbet story fails on every axis: improved range, blocked folds, no rep.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7d completes 8-6 (6-7-8-9-T) inside villain's peel range and fills the board's low connection. Meanwhile the turn A -- the card hero leaned on to barrel -- made top pair for the Ax hands that called, so the range checking to hero on the river is stronger than it was a street earlier.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain holds Ax top pairs that called the turn on purpose, Tx/9x that refuse to fold, and the rivered straight. The folding region is busted QJ/KJ/AQ-without-a-pair -- and hero blocks the first two.
explanation.handLogic: King-high, no pair, no showdown value. The overbet "rep AK/AA" temptation fails the story test: hands that strong mostly bet this river themselves earlier in the range tree, and villain's actual calling region (Ax) is precisely the one that never folds to the rep.
explanation.sizingLogic: The overbet is the punished choice at 30 BB of stake: maximum money behind a story the calling range does not believe. Smaller bluffs share the flaw at lower cost.
explanation.commonMistake: Escalating the bluff size when the earlier barrels did not work -- the range that kept calling is telling you it connected.
explanation.takeaway: A bluff needs a believable story AND an audience that can fold. Missing both, the only professional line is the free showdown you cannot win -- check and lose the minimum.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero blocks AJ-type floats villain rarely has and none of the pairs; removal is irrelevant next to the showdown math: ace-high beats every busted draw and loses to every pair.

question.prompt: Flop Qh 8s 3d; turn Kc; river 4s. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Ah Jh?

explanation.short: Ace-high with real showdown value: betting turns a winner into a loser.
explanation.riverLogic: The 4s is a brick after hero barrelled the royal gutter on the K turn and missed. What remains is ace-high -- and on this runout that is a hand, not air: villain's busted JT/T9 gutters and missed backdoors all lose to it at showdown.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain's check-call check-call range is Qx and 8x pairs plus those busted draws. The pairs never fold to one more bet at a workable price, and the draws hero beats fold to any bet -- so a bluff folds out only the hands hero already beats.
explanation.handLogic: This is the defining showdown-value shape: beat all the folds, lose to all the calls. Betting any amount converts a hand that wins the busted-draw showdowns into a bluff that isolates against pairs.
explanation.sizingLogic: No size fixes the shape. The big barrel is the expensive version -- 20 BB behind a bet that only better hands call -- hence the critical grade.
explanation.commonMistake: Counting ace-high as "no showdown value" and auto-barrelling: against capped, draw-heavy check-call ranges, ace-high IS the bluff-catcher-beater.
explanation.takeaway: Before turning a missed draw into a bluff, ask what you beat at showdown. If the answer is "everything that folds", check.

scenarios.blockerNote: The A blocks AK, the strongest Kx that pays nothing anyway; the Q blocks QJ/QT, the exact hands a thin bet would want to be called by. The removal argues against betting.

question.prompt: Flop Kd 7s 3c; turn Qh; river 7d. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Ac Qc?

explanation.short: Second pair on the paired river: the thin bet has no customers left.
explanation.riverLogic: The 7d pairs the board -- the same runout BB students face from the other seat against a small bet. From the bettor's chair the lesson inverts: the 7 promotes villain's 7x mid-pairs to trips, and the thin-value bet AQ wanted to make just lost its market.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain's check-call check-call range is Kx (never folding, always beating AQ), 7x that just made trips, and Qx worse than hero's -- but hero's own Q blocks most of that last region. What is left to call a bet and lose? Almost nothing.
explanation.handLogic: AQ still beats the busted gutters (65-type) and the weaker Qx at showdown; checking realizes that equity for free. Betting collects from a region hero mostly blocks while paying off Kx and the new trips.
explanation.sizingLogic: The small bet is the designated temptation here (stake basis: small): it looks like a value bet but the callers-worse are blocked and the callers-better multiplied. Bigger sizes only deepen the error.
explanation.commonMistake: Betting "thin value" by hand strength alone: value is defined by who calls, and the paired river rewrote that list.
explanation.takeaway: A paired river can delete your thin-value market. Recount the callers before you bet -- and when they are gone, take the showdown.

scenarios.blockerNote: The T blocks a share of the JT/T9 busted draws hero beats at showdown -- mildly unwelcome -- but the core logic is unchanged: ace-high beats the folds and loses to the calls.

question.prompt: Flop Qh 8s 3d; turn Kc; river 4s. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. BTN holds As Ts and the best line is to check back. WHY?

explanation.short: Ace-high beats every hand that would fold: the check realizes real equity.
explanation.riverLogic: Hero barrelled the gutter to the jack on the K turn and bricked. Ace-high remains, and against villain's check-call check-call range -- Qx, 8x, busted JT/T9 -- it wins exactly the showdowns against the busted draws.
explanation.rangeContext: The "thin value" story fails on contact: the hands below ace-high that might call are 8x -- and 8x is a PAIR; it beats ace-high. There is no worse hand that calls, so there is no value bet, at any size.
explanation.handLogic: Beats all busted draws (they fold to any bet -- so betting wins nothing extra from them); loses to all pairs (they call -- so betting loses extra to them). The check dominates.
explanation.sizingLogic: Sizing is irrelevant to a structurally wrong bet; the polar option merely maximizes the damage. The trap-risk reason is the right ACTION with the wrong logic -- ace-high fears no check-raise; it fears the call.
explanation.commonMistake: Believing "8x calls and we beat it": 8x is a pair of eights; ace-high does not beat it. Verify the ladder before labelling a bet thin value.
explanation.takeaway: No worse hand calls, no better hand folds: the two-question test that ends most river mistakes before they happen.

scenarios.blockerNote: With the Qd and Th on the board, villain's QT starts at 3x3 = nine combos; hero's Td cuts it to 3x2 = six. QT is the top-pair hand that calls a small bet and wins, so trimming a third of it is the nudge that keeps the thin bet alive alongside the check.

question.prompt: Flop Qd 7c 4h; turn Th; river 2d. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with Ah Td?

explanation.short: Second pair top kicker at the boundary: small bet and check split the combo.
explanation.riverLogic: The 2d is a brick on a runout with no straights and no flush. Hero's pair of tens with the ace kicker sits exactly at the line between thin value and showdown-taking.
explanation.rangeContext: The callers-worse are T9s/T8s (same pair, worse kicker) and stubborn 7x; the callers-better are Qx and the JJ-type overpairs-to-the-ten that check-called twice. The two pools are close in weight, and hero's own T thins the QT slice of the better pool.
explanation.handLogic: AT beats the busted gutters and the worse Tx; it loses to any Qx and JJ. Neither betting small nor checking is an error -- the combo genuinely splits.
explanation.sizingLogic: If betting, small only: the worse-Tx-and-7x pool calls a third of pot and vanishes against anything larger, while Qx calls everything. Big sizing is the one clearly losing line.
explanation.commonMistake: Forcing a pure answer where the ranges do not give one -- and especially "betting bigger to find out", which pays the better hands to tell you.
explanation.takeaway: When callers-worse and callers-better balance, both small-bet and check are professional; note the blocker that tips you and move on.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero's two 8s remove the 8x hands villain would call with lightest, tilting the calling range toward Kx/Qx broadways -- which argues for the bigger size, not the smaller one.

question.prompt: Flop 8d 5c 2h; turn Kc; river Qh. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. What is BTN best action with 8s 8h?

explanation.short: Top set, and the runout delivered customers: the small bet is the leak here.
explanation.riverLogic: The K turn and Q river look scary but they are hero's gift: villain's check-call range was full of overcard broadways (KJ, KT, QJ, QT, KQ) that just made pairs and two pair. No straight completes (the J-T bridge never arrives) and no flush exists.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain now holds Kx and Qx pairs plus KQ two pair -- all genuine bluff-catchers against hero's line, all beaten by the flopped set. Nothing beats hero: KK and QQ three-bet preflop in the baseline, no straight or flush is possible, and the board never pairs -- top set is the effective nuts with zero combos above it, which is why checking it back grades as a full punt.
explanation.handLogic: Top set beats every pair and every two pair on the final board. The value question is not whether to bet -- it is how much the new Kx/Qx pairs will pay.
explanation.sizingLogic: They pay big: hands that just paired on the last two cards after calling two streets do not fold to 75%. The small bet -- fine on some rivers -- is THE mistake on this one: it charges the richest calling range of the whole tree a bottom-tier price. Overbet is a reasonable polar alternative.
explanation.commonMistake: Reflex-sizing down "because the board ran out high" -- the high cards are what built villain's calling range.
explanation.takeaway: Size to the range the runout created, not to the flop you remember: when the scare cards hit VILLAIN's range, bet bigger.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero's Q blocks QJ/QT -- unfortunately those are the CALLERS a value bet wants, which is exactly why the size must stay small enough to keep the rest of the pool in.

question.prompt: Flop Qs 8h 3c; turn 2d; river 9h. BTN c-bet flop, BB called; BTN barrelled turn, BB called; BB now checks the river. BTN holds Ad Qd and the best line is a small bet. WHY?

explanation.short: TPTK after the 9 completes JT: merge small, do not polarize into the improved region.
explanation.riverLogic: The 9h completes 8-9-T-J-Q for JT (which peeled a gutter on the flop) and promotes Q9 to two pair. AQ is still the best one-pair hand and still beats the Qx ladder -- but the hands that improved are precisely the ones a big bet isolates against.
explanation.rangeContext: Villain's river checks contain QJ/QT (call a modest bet, lose), Q9/JT (call anything, win), 8x and busted gutters (fold to everything). The composition screams merge: there is a real but price-sensitive value market.
explanation.handLogic: AQ beats QJ, QT and the stubborn 8x that peels a small bet; it loses to Q9, JT and slow-played sets. Small keeps the first group in; any polar size trades them away for the second.
explanation.sizingLogic: Merged small (~33%) is the only size whose calling pool is majority-worse. "Overbet -- TPTK is the nuts here" fails (JT/Q9/sets exist); "check -- the 9 kills all value" over-corrects (QJ/QT still pay); "bet big to charge draws" is a category error -- there are no draws on a river.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-sizing a merged hand: the bigger the bet, the purer the calling range that beats you.
explanation.takeaway: After a connecting river, downshift: the merge price keeps the dominated callers your hand was built to beat.
